name: Single-Leg Sit-to-Stand
description: Sit on the front edge of a sturdy chair, extend one leg out in front, and stand up using only the planted leg, then lower back down under control. The chair provides a safe target and depth guide; keep it against a wall so it cannot slide.
rationale: Sit-to-stand is one of the best-validated functional exercises for the lower limb: performance on timed sit-to-stand tests (5-times STS, 30-second STS) reliably reflects lower-extremity strength and power and predicts fall risk in older adults, and progressive sit-to-stand training has been shown to improve leg strength, functional mobility, and gait in older and deconditioned populations. Loading a single leg substantially raises the demand on the quadriceps and hip extensors and exposes side-to-side asymmetry, which is directly relevant after knee or hip injury/surgery and for anyone who struggles to rise from low seats or climb stairs. Direct randomized evidence specifically for the single-leg variant is limited, so this is best framed as a targeted progression of a well-supported movement: the bilateral sit-to-stand evidence is moderate, and the unilateral overload rationale (closed-chain, task-specific quadriceps and gluteal strengthening with a built-in range limit) is mechanistically sound and easily dosed at home.

muscleRoles.notes: Concentrically extends the knee to stand and eccentrically controls the lowering back to the chair; principal mover of the single stance leg.
Primary hip extensor driving the stance leg to upright.
Knee extensor important for terminal extension and patellar tracking on the single stance leg.
Major knee extensor contributing much of the extension force.
Deep knee extensor working with the other vasti.
Biarticular; assists knee extension but slackened at the flexed hip in the seated start, so it contributes less than the vasti.
Assist hip extension and resist anterior tibial shear at the knee during the rise.
Ischiocondylar (hamstring) portion assists hip extension from the flexed start position.
Controls frontal-plane pelvis/hip position and prevents knee valgus on the single stance leg — heightened demand in the unilateral variant.
Assists gluteus medius in hip/pelvis stabilization during single-leg loading.
Controls the tibia over the foot for ankle/balance stability throughout the movement.
Maintains forward tibial control and balance over the stance foot during single-leg loading.
Keeps the trunk upright and controls the forward lean needed to rise.
Provides core/trunk stability during the loaded transition.

cues.text: Sit tall at the front edge of the chair, plant the working foot flat and slightly behind the knee, and lift the other leg off the floor.
Hinge forward from the hips (nose over toes) to load the stance leg, then drive through the whole foot to stand.
Keep your kneecap tracking over your second toe - do not let the knee collapse inward.
Lower slowly for a count of three, tapping the chair lightly rather than dropping onto it.

regressions.name: Assisted single-leg sit-to-stand
regressions.description: Use fingertips on a countertop, wall, or the chair arms to share load and help balance, reducing the demand on the stance leg.

regressions.name: Higher seat / partial single-leg
regressions.description: Raise the seat with a firm cushion or use a taller chair so the range and load are smaller; progress the height down over time. Bilateral sit-to-stand is the full regression if single-leg is not yet possible.

progressions.name: Eccentric-focused (lower-only) single-leg
progressions.description: Stand on two legs, then lower to the chair slowly on one leg over 4-5 seconds to emphasize eccentric quadriceps and gluteal control.

progressions.name: Weighted or lower-seat single-leg
progressions.description: Hold a dumbbell, kettlebell, or loaded backpack at the chest, and/or lower the seat height to increase range and force demand.

dosing: 3 sets of 5-8 controlled reps per leg, 2-3 days per week, with a 2-3 second lowering phase; rest ~60-90 seconds between sets. Progress by lowering seat height, adding external load, or shifting to eccentric-only reps as strength and control improve.

name: Wall Angel
description: Stand with your back, hips, and head lightly touching a wall, arms raised into a goalpost with the backs of the hands and forearms pressed to the wall. Slowly slide the arms overhead and back down while keeping every contact point on the wall.
rationale: Wall angels are used in the clinic to retrain the scapular upward rotators (lower trapezius and serratus anterior) and posterior rotator cuff while lengthening the anterior chest and internal rotators, addressing the protracted/anteriorly-tilted "rounded shoulder" posture linked to subacromial pain and neck/upper-back complaints. The wall gives real-time feedback: it constrains compensations (shrugging, lumbar hyperextension, arms drifting off) and forces the movement to occur through genuine glenohumeral elevation with scapular upward rotation and thoracic extension. Evidence is mechanistic and indirect rather than from trials of "wall angels" by name: EMG work on the closely related wall-slide maneuver shows meaningful serratus anterior and lower-trapezius recruitment with low upper-trapezius activity (a favorable ratio for scapular dyskinesis), and scapular-stabilizer strengthening programs improve pain and function in subacromial pain syndrome. It is best framed as a low-load postural/mobility and motor-control drill; there is no strong RCT support for it as an isolated intervention, so expectations should stay modest.

muscleRoles.notes: Drives scapular upward rotation and depression as the arms elevate, counteracting the tendency to shrug; a targeted mover in this drill.
Produces scapular upward rotation and posterior tilt, keeping the scapula flush against the ribcage/wall through overhead range.
Assists scapular retraction to hold the arms/forearms back onto the wall.
Retracts and helps stabilize the scapula against the wall.
Maintains glenohumeral external rotation so the backs of the hands/forearms stay on the wall.
Assists external rotation and posterior cuff stabilization during elevation.
Helps pin the arms into horizontal abduction/external rotation against the wall.
Contributes to the upward-rotation force couple but is deliberately kept quiet to avoid shrugging.
Maintains thoracic extension and upright trunk contact without arching the low back.
Stretched across the chest as the arms are held back and slid overhead.
Lengthened as the humerus is held in external rotation and horizontal abduction against the wall.
Internal rotator/adductor stretched as the arms elevate overhead in external rotation.
Internal rotator lengthened by the sustained external-rotation position.

cues.text: Set your head, upper back, and tailbone against the wall, then gently draw your ribs down so your low back is only lightly arched, not jammed away from the wall.
Keep the backs of your hands and your forearms 'painting' the wall the whole time, like making a snow angel.
Slide the arms up only as far as you can go without the wrists lifting off, the shoulders shrugging up to your ears, or your low back popping forward.
Move slowly, about 3 seconds up and 3 seconds down, breathing normally rather than holding your breath.

regressions.name: Seated or hands-off wall angel
regressions.description: Perform the same arm path standing a few inches away from the wall (or seated), keeping the trunk tall but only lightly grazing the wall, reducing the range and contact demand for stiff shoulders.

regressions.name: Partial-range goalpost holds
regressions.description: Move only through the lower half of the range (from goalpost to just above the head) and hold briefly, staying in the range where wall contact can be maintained.

progressions.name: Weighted or banded wall angel
progressions.description: Hold a light dowel, small weight, or loop a resistance band across the forearms to add external-rotation and upward-rotation demand while maintaining full wall contact.

progressions.name: Foam-roller wall angel
progressions.description: Lie on a foam roller placed lengthwise along the spine and perform the angel motion against gravity, greatly increasing the scapular-stabilizer control challenge without wall feedback.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 8-10 slow, controlled reps, 4-6 days per week; hold the top position 1-2 seconds and only work through the range you can keep full wall contact.
emgNotes: Modeled on wall-slide EMG findings showing relatively high serratus anterior and lower-trapezius activation with a low upper-trapezius-to-lower-trapezius ratio; upper trapezius should stay quiet (no shrug).

name: Countertop Incline Push-Up
description: A scaled push-up performed with both hands planted shoulder-width on the edge of a kitchen countertop while the feet stay back on the floor, creating an inclined body angle. The raised hand position reduces the percentage of body weight the arms must press, so the patient lowers the chest toward the counter and pushes back up under control. The higher and more vertical the counter, the easier the load — making it an ideal home starting point before progressing toward the floor.
rationale: Incline (hands-elevated) push-ups let a patient train the pressing pattern — pectoralis major, anterior deltoid, triceps, and serratus anterior — at a self-scalable fraction of body weight, which is why they are a mainstay of home upper-body and closed-chain shoulder progressions when floor push-ups are too hard or provoke symptoms. EMG and kinematic studies of push-ups consistently show that raising the hands lowers the ground-reaction force and pectoral/triceps demand, giving a graded way to rebuild pressing strength; the closed-chain hand-fixed position also recruits serratus anterior, supporting scapular upward rotation and reducing scapular winging, which is relevant to shoulder-rehab and impingement programs. General resistance-training evidence supports progressive loading of these muscle groups for strength and hypertrophy, and closed-chain push-up variants are widely used post shoulder injury for co-contraction and joint stability. Evidence for the countertop variant specifically is largely extrapolated from broader push-up and closed-chain research rather than trials of this exact height, so the benefit is best described as strong mechanistic/functional support with moderate direct evidence.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover for the press (shoulder flexion/horizontal adduction), driving the chest away from the counter. Correct as primary.
Extends the elbows to press the body back up. Correct as primary.

Downgraded from primary. Anterior deltoid is a genuine and strong contributor to the push-up press, but in EMG terms it consistently trails pec major and triceps; labeling three co-equal primaries overstates its share. Secondary (assisting mover) is the honest role.
Closed-chain, hands-fixed position loads serratus for scapular protraction/upward rotation and anti-winging. Synergist is appropriate (would be closer to primary only in an explicit push-up-plus).
Assists triceps with terminal elbow extension. Correct as synergist.
Isometrically maintains a rigid plank-like trunk; no dynamic trunk movement, so stabilizer (not a mover) is correct.
Deep abdominal bracing to resist trunk sag. Correct as stabilizer.
Co-contracts to hold hip extension and keep the body in a straight line. Isometric anti-sag role, correct as stabilizer.

cues.text: Place both hands on the edge of the counter a little wider than shoulder width, then walk your feet back until your body forms a straight, gently angled line from head to heels.
Squeeze your glutes and brace your abs so your hips do not sag or pike — move as one solid plank.
Bend your elbows and lower your chest toward the counter over about two seconds, then press back up smoothly; keep elbows at roughly a 45-degree angle to your torso, not flared straight out.
At the top, gently push the counter away and spread your shoulder blades apart to engage the muscles that stabilize the shoulder blade.

regressions.name: Higher / more vertical surface
regressions.description: Perform the push-up against a wall or a tall chest-height surface instead of the counter. The more upright your body, the less body weight your arms support, making the press easier for painful or deconditioned shoulders.

regressions.name: Reduced range of motion
regressions.description: Lower only partway toward the counter, stopping before end range, and press back up. Shortening the range keeps the load lighter and pain-free while you build tolerance.

progressions.name: Lower surface incline push-up
progressions.description: Move your hands to a progressively lower surface such as a sturdy chair seat or a step. Lowering the hand height increases the share of body weight the arms must press, adding load.

progressions.name: Countertop push-up plus / floor push-up
progressions.description: Add an extra scapular protraction 'plus' at the top of each rep, or progress to a knee or full push-up on the floor once countertop reps feel easy, to increase serratus and pressing demand.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 8-12 controlled repetitions, resting 60-90 seconds between sets, 2-3 non-consecutive days per week. Choose a counter height at which the last 1-2 reps feel challenging but form stays perfect and the shoulder is pain-free; progress to a lower surface once you can complete 3 sets of 12 with good control.
emgNotes: Push-up EMG studies show pectoralis major, anterior deltoid, and triceps brachii as prime movers, with serratus anterior highly active in the closed-chain hand-fixed position (especially with an added protraction 'plus'). Raising the hands (incline) reduces ground-reaction force and overall muscle activation relative to a floor push-up, providing a lower-load version of the same recruitment pattern.

name: Knee-to-Wall Ankle Mobilization
description: Stand facing a wall in a short lunge with the front foot a few inches away, keep that heel firmly planted, and drive the knee straight forward until it touches (or reaches toward) the wall to load the ankle into dorsiflexion. The wall gives both the mobilizing surface and a fixed reference to measure toe-to-wall distance.
rationale: Limited ankle dorsiflexion is one of the most common mechanical restrictions after a lateral ankle sprain, in Achilles/patellar tendinopathy, and following any period of immobilization, and it drives compensations such as early heel rise, excessive foot pronation, knee valgus, and reduced squat depth. The knee-to-wall (weight-bearing lunge) position is the same posture used in the Weight-Bearing Lunge Test, which has good-to-excellent intra- and inter-rater reliability for quantifying dorsiflexion, so patients can self-measure progress in centimeters or degrees. Weight-bearing dorsiflexion self-mobilization has moderate evidence for increasing dorsiflexion ROM in people with chronic ankle instability and post-sprain, and because it loads the talocrural joint through range under body weight it targets the posterior capsule and calf tissue in a functional, closed-chain manner that transfers to gait and squatting. Because the knee is bent, gastrocnemius is slackened and the stretch/mobilization bias shifts toward soleus, the deep posterior compartment, and the joint itself rather than being a pure gastroc stretch. Evidence is graded moderate: real reliability data support the test position and there is supportive (though not definitive, largely small-trial) evidence for the mobilization improving ROM.

muscleRoles.notes: Agonist dorsiflexor. In closed-chain, it contracts to help draw the tibia forward over the fixed foot. Defensible as primary in the agonist sense, though the forward knee travel is largely body-weight/momentum driven, so its concentric contribution is modest for what is fundamentally a mobilization.
Assists closed-chain dorsiflexion as the ankle loads forward.
Assists dorsiflexion alongside tibialis anterior.
Primary tissue target: knee flexion slackens gastrocnemius so the dorsiflexion stretch is biased onto soleus and the deep posterior compartment.
Lengthened at the ankle but its stretch is reduced by the flexed knee — correctly de-emphasized versus a straight-knee calf stretch.
Deep posterior compartment plantarflexor stretched as the talus and tibia move into dorsiflexion.
Deep posterior plantarflexor lengthened at end-range dorsiflexion.
Deep posterior plantarflexor lengthened with dorsiflexion loading.
Controls the forward-traveling knee and supports body weight over the loaded ankle.

cues.text: Keep your heel glued to the floor the entire time — if it lifts, the ankle stops moving and the drill is lost.
Drive your knee straight forward over the middle toes toward the wall; don't let it drift inward.
Keep the arch of your foot lifted so the movement comes from the ankle, not by collapsing the foot flat.
Use the wall as a ruler: touch it, then step the foot back a little farther each session as range improves.

regressions.name: Foot closer to wall
regressions.description: Start with the toes only an inch or two from the wall so the knee can reach it while the heel stays down; a shorter lever means less demand on ankle range.

regressions.name: Seated / non-weight-bearing dorsiflexion
regressions.description: If bearing weight is painful (e.g., acute sprain), pull the ankle into dorsiflexion seated with a strap or band instead, removing body-weight load while still working range.

progressions.name: Increase toe-to-wall distance
progressions.description: Progressively step the foot farther from the wall so the knee must travel through greater dorsiflexion to make contact, increasing the mobilization demand.

progressions.name: Banded joint mobilization (Mulligan-style)
progressions.description: Loop a resistance band around the front of the ankle just below the malleoli with the anchor behind, adding a posterior glide of the tibia on the talus while performing the knee-to-wall reach.

dosing: Perform on the affected (or more restricted) side: 2-3 sets of 10-15 slow reps, holding 2-3 seconds at end-range contact, once or twice daily. Also usable as a pre-squat/pre-run warm-up. Work into a firm stretch or joint-tension sensation, never sharp pinch at the front of the ankle.

name: Legs-Up-the-Wall
description: A restorative pose (Viparita Karani): lie on your back with your buttocks close to a wall and rest your straight legs vertically up the wall, arms relaxed at your sides, breathing slowly for 5-15 minutes. The wall passively supports the legs so no muscular effort is needed.
rationale: Legs-Up-the-Wall is used in home routines for three linked purposes, all with modest published support. (1) Passive leg elevation above heart level assists venous and lymphatic return, which can transiently reduce lower-limb swelling and the sensation of heavy, tired legs after prolonged standing; limb elevation is a long-established, physiologically sound component of edema management. (2) The supported supine posture paired with slow diaphragmatic breathing promotes parasympathetic activation and downregulation, and restorative yoga/slow-breathing practices show small-to-moderate benefits for perceived stress, relaxation, and sleep onset in the general wellness literature. (3) With legs straight it provides a low-load static hamstring and calf stretch. It is safe, requires only a wall, and has essentially no strength demand, making it a good recovery/wind-down or pre-sleep option. Evidence is limited and largely mechanistic or from small wellness studies rather than high-quality trials, and it is not a treatment for venous disease; patients with uncontrolled hypertension, glaucoma, or late pregnancy should modify or avoid the inversion.

muscleRoles.notes: In a passive restorative posture with no strength demand, the diaphragm is the main working muscle: slow diaphragmatic breathing drives the parasympathetic/relaxation effect that is the exercise's purpose. 'Primary' here reflects breathing, not locomotion.
Two-joint hamstrings are stretched at both ends: hip flexion (legs elevated) plus knee extension places them on gentle passive stretch. Correct.

Mild passive lengthening, driven mainly by knee extension (gastroc crosses the knee). Note: in the relaxed supine position the ankle tends to rest in slight plantarflexion, not dorsiflexion, so the original note's dorsiflexion claim is inaccurate; stretch is modest and comes chiefly from the extended knee, not ankle dorsiflexion.
Sustained hip flexion with pelvis near the wall places gluteus maximus (a hip extensor) on gentle passive stretch. Correct.

Weakest of the lengthening assignments. Elevated hips promote posterior pelvic tilt and flattening of the lumbar lordosis (mild lumbar flexion), giving the lumbar extensors light lengthening. Effect is mostly relaxation/unloading rather than true end-range stretch, but a lengthening tag is defensible.

cues.text: Sit sideways with one hip touching the wall, then roll onto your back as you swing both legs up the wall.
Slide your buttocks as close to the wall as is comfortable; the closer you are, the more stretch you feel in the backs of the legs.
Let the wall do all the work - completely relax your legs, feet, and shoulders so there is no muscular effort.
Breathe slowly and low into the belly, making each exhale a little longer than the inhale.

regressions.name: Bent-knee / hips away from wall
regressions.description: Scoot the buttocks a few inches from the wall and allow a soft bend in the knees to reduce hamstring stretch and any low-back strain.

regressions.name: Legs on a chair (90/90)
regressions.description: Rest the lower legs on a chair seat with hips and knees at 90 degrees instead of vertical - achieves leg elevation and relaxation without the flexibility demand or full inversion.

progressions.name: Hips close with active dorsiflexion
progressions.description: Move the buttocks flush to the wall and gently pull the toes down toward you (dorsiflex) to add a stronger calf and hamstring stretch.

progressions.name: Supported inversion with pelvis elevated
progressions.description: Place a firm folded blanket or bolster under the pelvis to raise the hips higher, increasing the inversion/venous-return effect while extending the hold time.

dosing: Hold 5-15 minutes, once daily or as needed (e.g., end of day or before sleep). Come out slowly by bending the knees and rolling to one side to avoid dizziness.
emgNotes: Predominantly a passive/restorative posture with very low muscle activation throughout; the legs are supported by the wall rather than by active contraction. No exercise-specific EMG data are established.

name: Wall Hamstring Stretch
description: Lie on your back in a doorway with the leg to be stretched raised and resting straight against the wall or door frame; the other leg extends flat through the doorway. Gravity and the wall provide a low-load, sustained hamstring stretch with no equipment beyond a doorway.
rationale: Indicated for reduced hamstring extensibility and limited straight-leg-raise range, which is common with low back pain, prolonged sitting, and post-injury stiffness. Static stretching reliably increases hamstring length and joint ROM: holds around 30 seconds produce measurable extensibility gains, and this is the best-supported benefit (moderate evidence). The supine doorway setup is preferable to toe-touch or standing stretches because the trunk and pelvis are supported on the floor, which reduces lumbar flexion load and lets the person isolate the hamstring without straining the low back — useful for older adults or anyone with back sensitivity. Note that evidence for hamstring stretching preventing strains or improving performance is weak; the honest, well-supported reason to include it is targeted ROM/flexibility restoration and comfort in tasks requiring hip flexion with a straight knee (bending, reaching, gait).

muscleRoles.notes: Primary target; elongated across hip and knee as the raised leg is held in hip flexion with the knee straightening toward the wall. For a passive stretch, lengthening (not primary mover) is the correct role.
Medial hamstring stretched at both hip and knee. Redundant with the hamstrings group slug but valid.
Medial hamstring stretched at both hip and knee. Redundant with the hamstrings group slug but valid.
Lateral hamstring; long head crosses the hip, so it is stretched with hip flexion plus knee extension. Short head (knee-only) is correctly omitted since it does not lengthen at the hip.
Only recruited into the stretch if the ankle is actively dorsiflexed; secondary/conditional posterior-chain length. Kept as a defensible secondary lengthener, not a primary target.
Quad/knee-extensor activity holds the knee straight against the wall and can deepen the stretch via reciprocal inhibition. Synergist is appropriate for a stretch context.

Holds the hip-flexion position of the raised leg so the stretch stays on the hamstring rather than the low back. Could arguably be a synergist (active hip flexion holder), but stabilizer is defensible.

cues.text: Lie so your buttock is close to the door frame, then slide the working leg up the wall until you feel a firm but tolerable pull behind the thigh — not sharp pain.
Keep the raised knee straight; gently press the back of the knee toward the wall rather than letting it bend.
Let the opposite leg stay long and flat through the doorway so the pelvis and low back stay relaxed on the floor.
Pull your toes down toward your face to add a calf and nerve-tension component; ease off if you feel tingling.

regressions.name: Bent-knee doorway position
regressions.description: Rest the leg higher/closer with the knee slightly bent, or scoot the hips farther from the frame, to reduce hamstring tension for very tight or acutely irritable individuals.

regressions.name: Bent opposite knee
regressions.description: Bend the lower (non-stretching) leg with the foot flat on the floor to flatten the low back and remove any pull on the lumbar spine.

progressions.name: Hips closer with full knee extension
progressions.description: Move the buttocks nearer the wall and fully straighten the raised knee to increase the stretch through hip flexion and knee extension.

progressions.name: Add ankle dorsiflexion / active hold
progressions.description: Pull the toes toward you and add a gentle contract-relax (push the leg lightly into the wall for a few seconds, then relax into a deeper range) to bias the calf and use PNF-style gains.

dosing: Hold 30 seconds per repetition, 3-4 repetitions per leg, once or twice daily. Static holds of ~30 s are supported for ROM gains; longer or more frequent holds add little. Stretch to firm tension, never sharp pain, and breathe normally throughout.
emgNotes: Passive stretch with low target-muscle EMG; the hamstrings should be relaxed (minimal EMG) to allow lengthening, while light quadriceps activity may assist via reciprocal inhibition.

name: Doorway Lat Stretch
description: Standing in an open doorway, grip the frame or the top of the jamb with one hand at or above shoulder height, keep that elbow straight, then hinge the hips back and away from the anchored arm so the doorway holds the arm overhead while your bodyweight sinks the shoulder into flexion. You should feel a broad pull down the side of the back and armpit. Hold, breathe, and repeat on the other side.
rationale: Latissimus dorsi and teres major are large, two-joint muscles that adduct, extend, and internally rotate the shoulder; when short they cap overhead shoulder flexion and drive compensatory lumbar extension and rib flare during reaching (a common pattern in overhead athletes, desk workers with a stiff thorax, and post-lat-dominant lifters). Restoring lat extensibility is a standard component of restoring pain-free overhead elevation and is used clinically before loaded overhead work. Evidence specific to this named stretch is limited, but it rests on well-supported principles: static stretching reliably produces acute and, with regular practice, lasting gains in joint range of motion, and lengthening the lat/teres major directly targets the tissue that mechanically limits shoulder flexion. Its value is as a mobility and symptom-relief tool, not a strengthening or injury-prevention intervention, and gains require consistent repetition to persist. No outcome-specific trial is cited because high-quality trials isolating this stretch are lacking.

muscleRoles.notes: Primary target; hips-back hinge with arm anchored overhead elongates the lat across shoulder and thoracolumbar attachments. Correct as lengthening — for a passive stretch there is no true primary mover, so 'lengthening' is the right role.
Functional partner of the lat (shoulder adduction/extension/IR); elongated simultaneously, often felt at the posterior axilla. Endorsed.

Long head crosses the shoulder and is stretched with the arm in flexion. Net lengthening is modest because elbow extension shortens it distally, but as a secondary lengthening target this is acceptable.
Contingent on adding a side-bend away from the anchored arm, which lengthens the ipsilateral QL/lateral trunk. Reasonable as a secondary lengthening target.
Grip on the frame anchors the arm via a sustained isometric hold. Correct as stabilizer, not a mover.
Gentle core engagement keeps the hinge at the hips and prevents collapse into lumbar extension/rib flare. Correct as stabilizer.

cues.text: Anchor the hand at or just above shoulder height and keep that elbow fully straight so the pull travels into the lat, not the elbow.
Push your hips back and down away from the door rather than leaning your chest forward, letting bodyweight do the stretching.
Keep the lower ribs gently drawn down and the core lightly braced so you do not arch the low back to fake more range.
Exhale slowly and let the shoulder settle deeper on each breath; you want a firm stretch, never a sharp pinch at the front of the shoulder.

regressions.name: Hand lower on the frame
regressions.description: Grip the doorway at shoulder height instead of overhead and hinge only slightly, reducing shoulder flexion demand for stiff or painful shoulders.

regressions.name: Kneeling/seated variation at a low surface
regressions.description: Kneel and rest the forearm on a low table or counter, sinking the chest toward the floor to load the lat with far less overhead range and no balance demand.

progressions.name: Add trunk side-bend and rotation
progressions.description: From the overhead grip, side-bend and rotate the ribcage away from the anchored arm to bias the lower lat and QL and increase total tissue length.

progressions.name: Contract-relax (PNF)
progressions.description: At end range, gently pull the anchored arm down into the frame for 5-10 seconds, then relax and sink further, using autogenic inhibition to gain range.

dosing: Hold 30 seconds per side, 2-3 repetitions each side, 1-2 sessions per day. Progress total hold time toward 60 seconds per side as tolerance improves; stretch to a firm but comfortable end range with no sharp anterior shoulder pain.
emgNotes: No exercise-specific EMG data is cited here. Mechanistically this is a passive-lengthening task: the latissimus dorsi and teres major are placed on stretch by bodyweight while the grip (finger/wrist flexors) and trunk work isometrically to hold position, so target-muscle EMG should remain low rather than showing concentric activation.

name: Doorway Isometric Row
description: Stand in a doorway, grip both sides of the doorframe with elbows bent about 90 degrees, and pull as though rowing while the fixed frame prevents any motion — a pure isometric contraction of the pulling and scapular-retraction muscles.
rationale: This gives a patient a way to load the mid-back pulling chain (scapular retractors, lats, posterior deltoid, elbow flexors) with zero equipment and zero joint movement, which is valuable in two situations. First, in an irritable or early-stage shoulder/neck presentation where dynamic rowing is provoking, an isometric hold lets the patient build strength and get a training stimulus without moving through a painful arc; isometric strength gains are well established but are largely joint-angle-specific, so the hold should be trained near the position that matters. Second, for the very common desk-posture / rounded-shoulder pattern, strengthening the middle and lower trapezius and rhomboids is a reasonable, guideline-consistent component of managing nonspecific neck and shoulder pain, and this is an easy home progression of scapular-retraction training. Honest caveats: evidence for THIS specific drill is limited and extrapolated from the broader isometric-strengthening and scapular-strengthening literature rather than trials of a doorway row, and the analgesic effect of isometrics (studied mostly in lower-limb tendinopathy) is inconsistent — so it is offered as a low-risk strengthening and symptom-modulation option, not a proven standalone cure.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover of the shoulder-extension/adduction pull against the fixed frame.
Scapular retractor held isometrically through the pull; core of a rowing effort.
Prime scapular retractor emphasized by keeping the shoulder blades drawn back.
Assists shoulder extension/horizontal abduction in the row position. Highly active in horizontal rowing; secondary is acceptable but it borders on primary.
Assists retraction and depression; cued via drawing shoulder blades down to oppose shrugging.
Assists latissimus in shoulder extension/adduction.
Holds elbow flexion against the pull.
Elbow-flexion synergist stabilizing the ~90-degree elbow angle.
Assists elbow flexion in the neutral-forearm grip.
Rotator-cuff control of the glenohumeral joint during the hold.
Cuff stabilizer resisting internal rotation.
Braces the trunk so the pull does not become a lumbar lean-back.
Maintains upright neutral spine against the rowing effort.
Antagonist held in a lengthened position as the scapulae retract and the chest opens (static, not dynamically stretched).
Antagonist held lengthened as the shoulder sits in extension/retraction.

cues.text: Stand tall in the middle of an open doorway, elbows bent to about 90 degrees, and grip each side of the doorframe at roughly waist-to-lower-rib height.
Pull as if rowing the frame toward you and squeeze your shoulder blades down and back — the doorway won't move, so the effort stays isometric.
Keep your ribs down and shoulders away from your ears; do not let the effort turn into a shrug or a lean-back.
Breathe slowly and steadily through the hold rather than bracing and holding your breath.

regressions.name: Submaximal short hold
regressions.description: Pull at only 30-50 percent effort for 5-10 second holds, staying well under any symptom threshold while learning the scapular squeeze.

regressions.name: Higher hand position
regressions.description: Grip the frame closer to shoulder height, which shortens the lever and reduces demand on the lats and scapular retractors.

progressions.name: Single-arm doorway isometric row
progressions.description: Pull with one arm at a time against the frame, roughly doubling the load on that side's retractors and cuff.

progressions.name: Resistance-band dynamic row
progressions.description: Progress from the isometric hold to a full-range band row through the doorway to add movement and eccentric loading once symptoms allow.

dosing: Hold 5 repetitions of 20-30 seconds at a moderate-to-high, pain-free effort (roughly 60-80 percent), resting about 30-60 seconds between holds; perform 1-2 rounds, 3-5 days per week. For symptom modulation in an irritable shoulder, start at the lower effort and shorter holds and progress as tolerated.
emgNotes: No EMG data specific to this doorway variant; muscle roles are inferred from rowing/scapular-retraction biomechanics.

name: Chair-Supported Single-Leg Balance
description: Stand tall behind or beside a sturdy chair, resting your fingertips lightly on the chair back for support. Shift your weight onto one leg and lift the other foot just off the floor, holding a steady single-leg stance while the chair provides a safe touch-point to catch any loss of balance.
rationale: Single-leg stance time is a validated, independent predictor of falls and all-cause decline in older adults, and progressive static/dynamic balance training is one of the best-supported interventions for reducing fall rate and fall risk in community-dwelling seniors (this is a core, evidence-backed component of programs such as Otago and multicomponent exercise, reflected in Cochrane reviews of exercise for fall prevention). Training single-leg stance also improves ankle proprioception and neuromuscular control, which reduces recurrence after lateral ankle sprain. Anchoring the drill to a chair lets a patient overload balance safely at home by regulating how much fingertip support they use — starting with light touch and weaning toward no hands — so the challenge is graded to their actual capacity rather than their fear of falling. The chair converts an otherwise high-risk task into a self-progressable home exercise, which is why it is a staple early rung in home fall-prevention and lower-limb rehab programs.

muscleRoles.notes: Chief frontal-plane hip stabilizer; controls pelvic drop over the single stance leg.
Assists gluteus medius in stabilizing the pelvis on the femur.
Contributes to hip abduction and anterolateral pelvic control during stance.
Steadies the hip and controls femoral rotation on the planted leg.
Resists inversion sway and stabilizes the lateral ankle over the fixed foot.
Lateral ankle stabilizer countering medial postural sway.
Supports the medial arch and controls eversion/pronation sway.
Modulates anterior-posterior sway at the ankle.
Primary ankle plantarflexor for controlling forward sway during quiet stance.
Assists ankle control against forward sway.
Toe-gripping helps fine-tune balance corrections through the foot; minor contributor.
Lesser-toe flexion aids intrinsic balance adjustments over the base of support; minor contributor.
Maintains a stable, slightly-loaded knee on the stance leg.
Provides trunk/pelvic cylinder stability to keep the center of mass over the foot.
Keeps the trunk upright and resists lateral trunk lean during single-leg stance.

cues.text: Stand tall next to a sturdy chair and rest just your fingertips on the top of the backrest — enough to steady you, not to hold you up.
Pick a spot on the wall ahead and keep your eyes on it; imagine your standing foot spreading and gripping the floor like roots.
Level your hips — don't let the raised-leg side of your pelvis drop; keep a soft, unlocked knee on the standing leg.
If you can hold steady, lighten to one finger, then hover your hand a few inches above the chair, ready to catch yourself.

regressions.name: Two-hand chair hold, staggered stance
regressions.description: Hold the chair with both hands and keep the toes of the lifted leg lightly touching the floor behind you instead of fully lifting, reducing the balance demand.

regressions.name: Feet-together stance
regressions.description: Skip the single-leg lift entirely and practice standing with both feet together (or in tandem) at the chair, building steadiness before progressing to one leg.

progressions.name: Hands-free single-leg stance
progressions.description: Perform the hold with the hand hovering just off the chair, then with no hands, keeping the chair only as a safety catch.

progressions.name: Eyes-closed or head-turn balance
progressions.description: Once steady hands-free, close the eyes or add slow head turns to challenge the vestibular and proprioceptive systems while the chair remains within reach.

dosing: Hold 20-30 seconds per leg, 3-5 repetitions each side, 1-2 times daily. Progress by increasing hold time toward 30 seconds, reducing hand support, then adding the eyes-closed or head-turn variations. Always position the chair so it can be grabbed instantly if balance is lost.

name: Tandem Stance Balance
description: Stand with one foot placed directly in front of the other in a heel-to-toe line, so the heel of the front foot touches the toes of the back foot, and hold the narrowed stance while staying steady. No equipment is needed, though standing beside a wall or kitchen counter gives you something to touch for safety.
rationale: Tandem (heel-to-toe) stance is a narrowed base-of-support static balance task used to challenge and train postural control, and it is a validated rung on standardized balance measures — the Berg Balance Scale, the Short Physical Performance Battery, and the CDC STEADI progression (feet-together → semi-tandem → full tandem). Reduced tandem-stance hold time is associated with impaired balance and higher fall risk in older adults, which is why it appears in fall-risk screening. Balance and multicomponent exercise programs that include progressive static stances like this (e.g., the Otago Exercise Programme) have strong evidence from Cochrane reviews for reducing the rate of falls in community-dwelling older adults. As an isolated drill the specific RCT support is more limited, but the mechanistic rationale is sound: narrowing the base loads the ankle and hip frontal-plane stabilizers and forces integration of somatosensory, vestibular, and visual input for standing balance. Practical indications include fall-prevention programs, lower-limb proprioceptive deficits, ankle instability, and general balance retraining.

muscleRoles.notes: Primary ankle plantarflexor controlling anterior-posterior sway via the ankle strategy during quiet standing
Assists soleus in resisting forward sway
Checks backward sway and modulates dorsiflexion for balance corrections
Controls foot inversion and medial-lateral ankle stability in the narrow base
Everts the foot to resist lateral sway and keep weight over the base
Assists eversion for medial-lateral ankle control
Frontal-plane hip stabilizer resisting the hip-drop tendency in the narrowed stance
Assists gluteus medius in controlling medial-lateral pelvic sway
Maintains knee extension and postural rigidity of the stance limbs
Keeps the trunk upright over the base of support
Provides anticipatory trunk/core stiffness for postural control
Segmental spinal stabilization contributing to upright posture

cues.text: Place one foot directly in front of the other along an imaginary line so the front heel nearly touches the back toes.
Fix your eyes on a point at eye level and stand tall through the crown of your head.
Stay within arm's reach of a wall or counter so you can touch it if you start to tip.
Let small ankle sways correct your balance rather than taking a step; only touch down when you truly need to.

regressions.name: Semi-tandem stance
regressions.description: Offset the feet so the front heel sits alongside the big toe of the back foot instead of fully in line, widening the base and lowering the challenge.

regressions.name: Fingertip support
regressions.description: Rest one or two fingertips lightly on a wall or counter, reducing support as balance improves.

progressions.name: Eyes closed
progressions.description: Perform the same tandem hold with eyes closed to remove visual input and force greater reliance on ankle proprioception and the vestibular system.

progressions.name: Compliant surface
progressions.description: Stand on a foam pad, cushion, or folded towel to add somatosensory perturbation and increase the balance demand.

dosing: Hold the tandem position for 30 seconds, then switch which foot is forward; 3-4 holds per side, 1-2 times per day. Progress hold time or advance to a harder variation once you can hold 30 seconds steadily without touching support.

name: Heel-to-Toe Walk
description: Standing in a clear hallway, walk forward in a straight line placing the heel of the front foot directly in front of (and touching) the toes of the back foot each step, using a floor line (a tile seam or a strip of tape) as a visual guide and the wall for optional light fingertip support.
rationale: Heel-to-toe (tandem) walking narrows the base of support to roughly the width of one foot, which forces the ankle and hip frontal-plane stabilizers and the postural control system to manage a much larger medial-lateral balance demand than normal gait. It is a core component of validated balance and fall-risk assessments (Tinetti POMA, Berg Balance Scale, mini-BESTest), and dynamic balance/gait training of this type is a well-supported ingredient of multicomponent exercise programs that reduce falls in community-dwelling older adults (Cochrane and Otago-style program evidence). Clinically it is indicated for impaired dynamic balance, fall risk, post-ankle-sprain/chronic ankle instability reactive control, and general gait confidence. The specific drill in isolation has more mechanistic than trial-level support, so this is rated moderate: strong evidence exists for balance/gait training within programs, while the single-exercise dose-response is less precisely established.
equipment: floor line (tile seam or a strip of tape as a visual guide)

muscleRoles.notes: Controls frontal-plane pelvis/hip during single-leg support to prevent lateral sway on the narrow base. Correct: this is the dominant demand of tandem walking.
Assists gluteus medius in pelvic leveling during stance. Reasonable.
Reactive lateral ankle control resisting inversion on the narrowed line. Correct role.
Everts/stabilizes lateral ankle against roll-over. Correct.
Controls medial arch and inversion during single-limb support. Correct.
Dorsiflexes to clear swing foot and eccentrically controls lowering at heel contact. Synergist is defensible for the gait component.
Postural ankle muscle modulating plantarflexion torque to keep the tibia stable. Stabilizer framing is legitimate for a slow balance-dominant drill.
Plantarflexion push-off as weight transfers forward. Correct.
Hip extension propelling the body forward during stance. Reasonable for the gait cycle.
Controls knee extension in stance and absorbs load at contact. Reasonable.
Trunk/pelvic stability keeping center of mass over the narrow line. Correct.

cues.text: Place the heel of your front foot directly in front of and touching the toes of your back foot, so both feet stay on the single line.
Fix your eyes on a spot on the wall ahead rather than looking down at your feet.
Move slowly and pause for a beat in each heel-to-toe position before taking the next step.
Keep your torso tall and arms out slightly, ready to help you balance if you wobble.

regressions.name: Wide-line walk
regressions.description: Walk forward along the line with a normal, slightly narrowed stride instead of touching heel to toe, keeping a small gap between the feet for a wider base of support.

regressions.name: Wall-supported tandem
regressions.description: Perform the heel-to-toe steps while keeping one hand or fingertips on a hallway wall or counter for light support and confidence.

progressions.name: Eyes-closed tandem walk
progressions.description: Perform the heel-to-toe walk with eyes closed (near the wall for safety) to remove visual input and load the vestibular and proprioceptive systems.

progressions.name: Tandem walk with head turns
progressions.description: Add slow left/right head turns or up/down nods while walking heel-to-toe to challenge gaze stability and balance simultaneously.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 10-20 heel-to-toe steps, once or twice daily. Rest as needed between sets and always work within arm's reach of a wall or counter until steady.
emgNotes: No exercise-specific EMG data cited; muscle roles are assigned from gait and postural-control biomechanics (narrowed base of support increases frontal-plane hip abductor and ankle evertor/invertor demand).

name: Desk Wrist Flexor Stretch
description: Seated at a desk, place the palm flat on the desktop with the fingers pointing back toward the body and the elbow straight, then gently lean away from the hand to draw the wrist into extension and stretch the palm-side forearm flexors. The desk supplies the counter-pressure so the stretch is fully passive and easy to grade by how far you lean.
rationale: Prolonged keyboard and mouse use holds the wrist flexors in a shortened, low-grade-loaded position, and adaptive shortening/tension of these muscles is implicated in medial epicondylalgia (golfer's elbow), flexor-side forearm overuse, and general desk-related forearm discomfort. Static stretching of the wrist flexors reliably increases wrist-extension range of motion and is a standard, low-risk component of conservative programs and workplace micro-break routines for computer users. Honest caveat on strength of evidence: stretching alone has only limited-quality support for reducing epicondylalgia symptoms or preventing work-related upper-limb disorders (most workplace-exercise and epicondylopathy trials combine stretch with strengthening/eccentrics, so the isolated effect is hard to attribute), and there are no fabricated citations here. The defensible reasons to include it in a home routine are mechanistic and functional: it restores extensibility across the wrist and finger flexors, offloads the common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle, and gives a desk worker a fast, equipment-free way to interrupt sustained flexed postures. It pairs best with wrist-extensor strengthening rather than standing in for it.

muscleRoles.notes: Primary wrist flexor; wrist extension elongates it across the wrist. Correct target of the stretch.
Ulnar wrist flexor lengthened by wrist extension; shares the common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle.
When present, tensed and lengthened by combined wrist and finger extension. Correctly hedged for its variable presence.
Multi-joint flexor; stretch is maximized by extending the fingers as well as the wrist.
Deep multi-joint finger flexor; fully lengthened only when wrist and finger joints extend together.

Borderline but defensible: it does not cross the wrist, so a pure wrist-extension stretch barely loads it; only meaningfully lengthened if forearm supination is added. Note is honest about this caveat, so retained as a minor lengthening target.

cues.text: Sit tall at the desk, straighten the elbow, and place the palm flat on the desktop with the fingers pointing back toward your body.
Keeping the palm and fingers down, slowly lean your body away from the hand until you feel a gentle stretch along the palm side of the forearm.
Stop at a mild stretch, not pain; any tingling, numbness, or sharp pain in the hand means back off the range.
Keep the shoulder relaxed and breathe normally, holding the position steady rather than bouncing.

regressions.name: Soft-elbow version
regressions.description: Keep a slight bend in the elbow to reduce tension across the two-joint flexors, making the stretch gentler for irritable or acute forearms.

regressions.name: Fingers neutral
regressions.description: Rest only the palm on the desk with fingers relaxed (not pressed flat), reducing the multi-joint finger-flexor pull and the overall intensity.

progressions.name: Full elbow and finger extension
progressions.description: Lock the elbow completely straight and actively extend the fingers while leaning, lengthening the multi-joint flexors to their end range for a deeper stretch.

progressions.name: Contract-relax (PNF)
progressions.description: At the end range, gently press the palm down into the desk for 5 seconds, then relax and lean a little further into wrist extension to gain range.

dosing: Hold 20-30 seconds, 2-4 repetitions per side, 2-3 times per day; a good micro-break to repeat hourly during long desk sessions. Stretch to a gentle, tolerable pull with no pain or nerve symptoms.
emgNotes: Primarily a passive stretch: the wrist and finger flexors (flexor carpi radialis/ulnaris, flexor digitorum superficialis and profundus, palmaris longus) are under lengthening tension with minimal volitional activation, while the wrist extensors act only lightly to position or hold the joint when the stretch is taken actively. No high-force muscle work is intended.

name: Desk Wrist Extensor Stretch
description: Standing or sitting at a desk, place the back of the affected hand flat on the desk with the palm facing up and fingers pointing toward your body, elbow straight; gently press down and lean until you feel a stretch over the top of the forearm.
rationale: Targets the common wrist/finger extensor group (notably extensor carpi radialis brevis) whose enthesis at the lateral epicondyle is the pain source in lateral epicondylalgia (tennis elbow), and which becomes tight and symptomatic with sustained desk/keyboard and gripping work. Restoring extensor and posterior-forearm extensibility is a standard component of multimodal conservative care for lateral epicondylalgia and generic forearm/wrist overuse, and it is trivially self-administered at a desk for adherence. Honest evidence appraisal: stretching as an isolated intervention has only limited high-quality support — the strongest evidence for lateral epicondylalgia favors progressive loading (eccentric/heavy-slow resistance of the wrist extensors), with stretching typically studied as an adjunct within combined programs rather than a stand-alone cure. The defensible rationale here is mechanistic and functional (reduce extensor tension, provide symptom relief and a movement break during prolonged static desk postures), so it is best positioned as a low-risk complement to strengthening, not a substitute.

muscleRoles.notes: Primary target. Crosses the wrist; genuinely lengthened by wrist flexion with elbow extended and forearm pronated. Its lateral-epicondyle enthesis is the usual tennis-elbow pain source.
Wrist extensor/radial deviator; lengthened by the flexed-wrist position.
Ulnar wrist extensor lengthened by wrist flexion.
Multi-joint wrist/finger extensor lengthened across the wrist by wrist flexion (fingers relaxed).

Minor/incidental. Does not cross the wrist, so wrist flexion does not stretch it; it is only placed near end-range length by the elbow-extension + pronation position. Not the target, but the position does lengthen it.

cues.text: Keep the elbow fully straight and palm turned up (forearm pronated relative to a palm-down start) so the stretch reaches the top of the forearm, not just the wrist joint.
Let the fingers stay soft and slightly curled so the finger extensors also lengthen; a clenched fist reduces the stretch.
Ease into it slowly until you feel a mild pulling over the top of the forearm — a 3-4 out of 10, never a sharp or nervy pain.
Breathe normally and hold steady rather than bouncing; relax further on each exhale.

regressions.name: Self-hand assist, arm free
regressions.description: Instead of using the desk, hold the affected arm out in front, palm down, and use the opposite hand to gently draw the hand into flexion; control the intensity by hand so you can back off instantly if it is too strong.

regressions.name: Elbow slightly bent
regressions.description: Perform with the elbow softly bent rather than locked straight. This reduces tension on the two-joint forearm muscles and nerve tissue, making it gentler for acute or very tight/irritable cases.

progressions.name: Add ulnar deviation and finger flexion
progressions.description: From the stretch position, add gentle ulnar deviation (tilt the hand toward the little-finger side) and let the fingers curl further to bias the extensor carpi radialis brevis and finger extensors more specifically.

progressions.name: Progress to loaded extensor strengthening
progressions.description: Once pain-free, advance to progressive wrist-extensor loading (e.g., eccentric wrist extension / Tyler-twist type work), which carries the stronger evidence for lateral epicondylalgia, using this stretch as a warm-up or cool-down.

dosing: Hold 30 seconds per repetition, 3-4 repetitions, 2-3 times per day. Stretch to mild tension only (no sharp or nerve-like pain); can be done as a microbreak during prolonged desk/keyboard work.

name: Towel Row
description: Standing horizontal pull done at home by looping a towel around both handles of a sturdy closed door; hold an end in each hand, walk the feet in and lean back with arms straight, then pull the chest toward the door by driving the elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades together. Resistance is self-scaled by how far back you lean.
rationale: This is a self-regulated horizontal pulling exercise that lets a patient train the scapular retractors and posterior shoulder at home with no equipment beyond a towel and a door. Horizontal rowing is a well-studied way to load the middle and lower trapezius, rhomboids, and posterior deltoid: surface-EMG work on rowing variations consistently shows high activation of these scapular stabilizers, which is the rationale for including rows in programs for scapular dyskinesis, postural (upper-crossed) presentation, and general shoulder-girdle conditioning. Strengthening these muscles helps counterbalance dominant, often-tight anterior chest musculature and supports the balanced force-couples that upwardly rotate and stabilize the scapula. The row pattern itself has moderate published support; the specific towel-on-a-door variant has no dedicated trials, so its value rests on the transferable EMG/strengthening evidence for rowing plus its practical merit as an accessible, infinitely scalable home substitute for a band or cable row. It is a sound entry-level or bridge exercise, not a maximal-load one, because a towel/door caps the achievable resistance.

muscleRoles.notes: Retracts the scapula as the shoulder blades pull together; core mover of a horizontal row.
Prime scapular retractor, heavily loaded in horizontal rowing.
Drives shoulder extension/adduction to bring the trunk toward the door.
Produces shoulder extension and horizontal abduction during the pull; a true prime mover of the row.
Assists retraction and depresses/stabilizes the scapula, resisting shrugging.
Reclassified from secondary: its role here is glenohumeral centering of the humeral head, a rotator-cuff stabilizing function rather than a prime contributor to the pull.
Reclassified from secondary: posterior humeral-head stabilization/centering during the pull is a cuff stabilizer role, not a mover role.
Flexes the elbow during the pull phase.
Primary elbow flexor working through the pull.
Assists elbow flexion with the towel gripped in neutral.
Holds the trunk extended and braced against the leaning body angle.
Core bracing keeps the trunk-to-leg line rigid during the pull.
Antagonist stretched at the end of the pull as the scapulae retract and shoulders open.
Lengthened as the shoulder extends and horizontally abducts at the top of the row.

cues.text: Loop the towel around both handles of a fully closed, latched door, hold an end in each hand, and walk your feet toward the door so your body leans back with arms straight.
Pull your chest toward the door by driving your elbows back and down, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the end range.
Keep your shoulders away from your ears and your neck relaxed; don't shrug up as you pull.
Brace your abdomen and keep a straight line from heels to head; lower yourself back slowly under control rather than dropping.

regressions.name: Upright stance
regressions.description: Stand more vertically with feet farther from the door so you lean back less, reducing the load your arms and back must overcome.

regressions.name: Mid-range isometric hold
regressions.description: Pull to the mid-row position with shoulder blades squeezed and simply hold for 5-10 seconds, teaching retraction control before adding reps.

progressions.name: Single-arm towel row
progressions.description: Pull with one arm at a time to roughly double the demand on that side and add an anti-rotation core challenge.

progressions.name: Slow-tempo deep-lean row
progressions.description: Walk the feet farther in for a more horizontal body angle and lower yourself over a 3-4 second eccentric each rep to increase load and time under tension.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 10-15 controlled reps, resting about 60 seconds between sets, 2-3 days per week; adjust foot position so the last 2-3 reps of each set feel challenging but form stays clean.

name: Towel Scapular Retraction
description: Sit or stand tall and grip a rolled bath towel with both hands roughly shoulder-width apart, arms out in front at about chest height. Keeping the elbows slightly bent, pull outward on the towel as if trying to tear it while squeezing the shoulder blades down and back toward the spine. Hold the pinch, then slowly release. The towel gives a light, self-graded isometric resistance you can control by how hard you pull.
rationale: Scapular retraction training targets the middle and lower trapezius and rhomboids, the scapular stabilizers that are commonly weak or under-recruited relative to the upper trapezius and pectorals in people with rounded-shoulder posture, subacromial pain, and neck/upper-back pain. Strengthening the posterior scapular muscles and improving the serratus/lower-trap force couple is a core component of evidence-based rehab for subacromial pain syndrome and nonspecific neck pain, where scapular stabilization and periscapular strengthening programs reduce pain and improve function (multiple RCTs and systematic reviews support scapular exercise as part of shoulder and neck rehab). A towel row/tear is a very low-load, no-equipment entry point that lets a patient learn the retraction motor pattern and build early endurance at home before progressing to elastic-band or free-weight rowing; the direct pain- or posture-reduction evidence for this specific towel variant is extrapolated from the broader scapular-strengthening literature rather than tested in isolation, so the benefit is best described as moderate and primarily a motor-learning/low-load strengthening stepping stone.
bodyPosition: seated or standing, upright with a neutral spine

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover of scapular retraction, drawing the scapula toward the spine. Correct as primary.
Retracts and downwardly rotates/stabilizes the scapula against the towel. Correct as primary.
Assists retraction and adds depression to counter upper-trap dominance. Appropriate synergist.
Drives glenohumeral horizontal abduction as the hands pull outward on the towel. Correct synergist for a pull-apart pattern.
Externally rotates/centers the humeral head during the pull-apart. Valid stabilizer.
Assists external rotation and glenohumeral centering. Valid stabilizer.
Kept, but weakest link: it is cued to stay quiet rather than truly stabilizing; role is more of a coaching antagonist to avoid shrugging. Acceptable but borderline.
True antagonist protractor, eccentrically lengthened as the scapulae retract. Correct.
Anterior chest, a horizontal adductor lengthened during horizontal abduction/retraction. Correct.

cues.text: Pull the towel apart as if tearing it, and let that effort squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Think about drawing your shoulder blades down and back into your back pockets, not up toward your ears.
Keep your chin gently tucked and ribs down so the motion comes from the shoulder blades, not from arching the low back.
Hold the squeeze for 3 to 5 seconds, then release slowly with control.

regressions.name: Supported / lighter tear
regressions.description: Rest the forearms on a table or reduce how hard you pull on the towel, performing a gentle scapular squeeze with minimal load to learn the pattern pain-free.

regressions.name: Scapular setting without towel
regressions.description: Drop the towel and simply practice squeezing the shoulder blades together and down, holding briefly, to isolate the retraction motion before adding resistance.

progressions.name: Resistance band pull-apart
progressions.description: Swap the towel for an elastic band so the muscles work through a larger range with progressive tension as the band lengthens.

progressions.name: Bent-over or band row retraction
progressions.description: Progress to a rowing pattern (band or dumbbell) that loads scapular retraction dynamically through range against greater resistance.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 10-15 repetitions with a 3-5 second hold at end range, once daily or most days of the week; progress by increasing pull effort or hold time as the squeeze becomes easy and pain-free.
emgNotes: Isometric outward pull on the towel biases the middle/lower trapezius and rhomboids while keeping upper-trapezius activity low if shrugging is avoided; cueing scapular depression helps favor a lower-trap over upper-trap recruitment ratio.

name: Stair Calf Raise
description: Standing on a stair with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off, hold the handrail or wall for balance, press up onto your toes, then lower the heels below the step level for a full-range calf raise.
rationale: Plantarflexor (gastrocnemius/soleus) strength and endurance are strongly tied to walking speed, push-off power, and balance, and decline markedly with age and after lower-limb injury, making calf loading a high-value home exercise. Performing the raise off a stair edge lets the heel drop below the step, adding the dorsiflexion range a flat-floor raise cannot reach; this greater excursion loads the Achilles tendon through its full length and is the exact position used in the Alfredson eccentric heel-drop protocol, which has strong randomized-trial support for reducing pain and improving function in mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy. For general patients it restores calf capacity for stairs, walking, and single-leg push-off; the endurance version (single-leg heel-raise repetitions) is also a validated functional benchmark. Evidence is moderate: robust for eccentric/heavy calf loading in Achilles tendinopathy and for calf strength as a functional/fall-risk correlate, with the stair-edge variation being the standard delivery vehicle rather than a separately trialed exercise.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover for ankle plantarflexion driving the heel-raise; loaded most with the knee straight.
Prime plantarflexor; dominates when the knee is slightly bent and provides sustained push-off force.
Assists plantarflexion and controls the arch/inversion, keeping load off the medial ankle as the heel rises.
Helps plantarflex and stabilizes the great toe as you push through the ball of the foot.
Assists plantarflexion and grips the step through the lesser toes.
Assists plantarflexion and prevents the ankle rolling into inversion over the step edge.
Everts and weakly assists plantarflexion, balancing ankle position through the range.
Holds the knee position and keeps the leg steady during the standing raise.
Antagonist dorsiflexor that is stretched at the TOP of the raise when the ankle is fully plantarflexed; note it shortens (does not stretch) as the heel drops into dorsiflexion at the bottom.

cues.text: Rest only the balls of your feet on the stair edge with heels hanging free, and keep a light hand on the rail for balance, not for lifting.
Press slowly straight up onto your toes over about 2-3 seconds, feeling the calves shorten, and pause briefly at the top.
Lower under control until the heels sink below the step level and you feel a gentle stretch through the calf and Achilles, then reverse.
Keep the ankles tracking straight over the middle toes so you neither roll out nor collapse in as you rise and lower.

regressions.name: Flat-floor double-leg heel raise
regressions.description: Perform the raise on level ground without the heel drop, reducing range and balance demand while you build baseline calf strength.

regressions.name: Two-hand supported partial range
regressions.description: Hold the rail with both hands and limit the lower phase to level with the step, avoiding the deep stretch until tolerated.

progressions.name: Single-leg stair calf raise
progressions.description: Perform the full-range raise and heel drop on one leg, doubling the load through the working calf and Achilles.

progressions.name: Weighted or eccentric-emphasis raise
progressions.description: Hold a backpack/dumbbell, or rise on two legs and lower slowly on one, to load the eccentric phase Alfredson-style for tendon capacity.

dosing: General strengthening: 3 sets of 12-15 controlled reps (2-3 s up, brief hold, 2-3 s lower below the step), 4-5 days per week, progressing to single-leg or added load. Achilles tendinopathy (eccentric emphasis): work toward 3 sets of 15 heel drops, twice daily, per the Alfredson protocol, using mild-to-moderate tolerable discomfort as the guide.
emgNotes: Standing heel raises produce high gastrocnemius and soleus activation; the straight-knee position biases the gastrocnemius while a slightly flexed knee shifts emphasis to the soleus. Peronei and deep posterior compartment (tibialis posterior, long toe flexors) act as synergists/stabilizers, more so on the unstable step edge.

name: Eccentric Stair Descent
description: Standing at the top of a staircase, slowly lower yourself one step at a time, letting the trailing (top-step) leg bend under control to accept most of the load while the front foot touches down softly. Use the stair handrail for balance. The emphasis is on a slow, controlled descent (roughly 3-4 seconds per step) so the quadriceps of the supporting leg works eccentrically rather than dropping quickly.
rationale: Stair descent is one of the most demanding activities of daily living for the knee: it loads the quadriceps and patellar/quadriceps tendons eccentrically at high forces, and difficulty descending stairs is a hallmark complaint in patellofemoral pain, patellar tendinopathy, and knee osteoarthritis. Training the movement itself builds task-specific eccentric quadriceps strength and motor control, which carries over directly to safe, painless stairs at home. The mechanistic and clinical basis is well established — eccentric quadriceps loading (e.g. eccentric decline-squat protocols) has moderate published support for reducing pain and improving function in patellar tendinopathy, and eccentric/heavy-slow resistance work is a mainstay of tendinopathy rehab. In older adults, eccentric stair-descent capacity relates to lower-limb strength and fall risk, and controlled step-down training is commonly used to restore stair negotiation. Evidence specific to a graded stair-descent program is more limited than for isolated eccentric protocols, so the rating is set to moderate on the strength of the broader eccentric-loading and step-down literature rather than trials of this exact drill.

muscleRoles.notes: Correct primary mover. Contracts eccentrically to control knee flexion as the body lowers to the next step — the defining working action of stair descent.

Retained for its clinically relevant patellar-tracking role in PFP, but note this and the other vasti/RF are anatomical components of the quadriceps group already listed as primary — treat as granularity, not independent synergists, to avoid double-counting the prime mover.
Component head of the quadriceps; shares the eccentric knee-extensor load. Redundant with the quadriceps group entry but anatomically valid.
Component head of the quadriceps; shares eccentric knee-extensor load. Same double-counting caveat as the other vasti.
Biarticular quadriceps head assisting eccentric knee-extension deceleration; its excursion is limited because the hip is simultaneously flexing. Valid contributor.
Eccentrically controls hip flexion of the loaded stance leg during lowering, sharing shock absorption with the quadriceps. Appropriate as a mover/synergist.
Correct stabilizer role: frontal-plane pelvis/femur control preventing valgus collapse on the single stance leg.
Correct: eccentrically decelerates forward tibial progression and stabilizes the ankle during single-leg lowering.
Assists ankle/knee stabilization as the tibia advances over the foot. Reasonable stabilizer.
Co-contract with the quadriceps to stabilize the knee during loaded descent. Correct stabilizer role.
Controls the lowering forefoot (eccentric control of plantarflexion) for a soft, controlled touchdown. Reasonable stabilizer.

cues.text: Move slowly — take about 3 to 4 seconds to lower to the next step, resisting gravity the whole way.
Let the top-leg knee bend under control and keep it tracking over the second and third toes, not caving inward.
Touch the lower foot down softly and quietly, as if placing it on eggshells, rather than dropping onto it.
Keep a light hand on the handrail for balance and rest your weight into the supporting leg, not the rail.

regressions.name: Partial-range eccentric step-down
regressions.description: Lower only a few inches toward the next step and return, staying in a pain-free range and building tolerance before descending a full step height.

regressions.name: Two-hand rail support
regressions.description: Hold the handrail with both hands (or one hand plus a wall) to offload body weight and reduce balance demand while learning the slow tempo.

progressions.name: Hands-free slow descent
progressions.description: Perform the controlled descent without touching the handrail (keep it within reach) to increase single-leg balance and quadriceps demand.

progressions.name: Weighted eccentric descent
progressions.description: Hold a backpack or dumbbells, or descend two steps at a time, to raise the eccentric load on the quadriceps and tendon once form and pain allow.

dosing: 3 sets of 8-12 controlled step-downs per leg (each rep 3-4 seconds lowering), 4-6 days/week. In tendinopathy rehab, work into acceptable discomfort (roughly ≤4/10 pain that settles by the next morning) and progress load gradually over 8-12 weeks.
emgNotes: Descent phase is quadriceps-dominant and eccentric: the vasti and rectus femoris show high activity while lengthening to control knee flexion, with the loaded supporting leg carrying most of the demand. Gluteus medius activity rises to control frontal-plane knee position on the single stance leg, and gastroc/soleus and tibialis anterior modulate to control tibial progression and foot placement.

name: Backpack Weighted Carry
description: Load a sturdy backpack with books or water bottles, wear it snug on both shoulders with the load high and close to the spine, brace the trunk, and walk at a steady controlled pace for a set distance or time. The backpack is the only equipment needed, making this an accessible loaded-carry / "rucking" drill at home.
rationale: This is a functional loaded-carry (gait-under-load) task that trains the whole postural chain to resist fatigue while walking — directly transferable to daily activities like carrying groceries, a child, or luggage. The mechanistic and clinical basis is well established even where carry-specific trials are limited: (1) added axial/weighted-walking load is an osteogenic stimulus, and weighted-vest walking programs have shown benefit for bone-mineral density and postural strength in older and postmenopausal adults, so a graded weighted backpack is a low-cost home proxy; (2) walking with an anteriorly-compensated posterior load imposes sustained isometric demand on the spinal erectors, quadratus lumborum, and deep abdominal stabilizers, building the trunk-endurance that is repeatedly associated with reduced low-back-pain risk (McGill's endurance work); and (3) gait under load recruits the hip extensors and abductors for single-leg-stance control, supporting balance and lower-limb power relevant to fall prevention. It is scalable simply by changing pack weight, distance, or pace, which makes it practical for progressive home dosing. Evidence for the general strategy is moderate; evidence for the backpack specifically is more limited and mechanistic, so it is rated limited here.

muscleRoles.notes: Isometric anti-flexion control of the trunk against the posterior load throughout the walk — the featured trained tissue of a loaded carry.
Contributes hip-extension propulsion under load, but gait is submaximal for glute max — downgraded from primary to sit with the other gait propulsors.
Frontal-plane trunk control and pelvic stabilization during single-leg stance phases.
Maintains intra-abdominal pressure and lumbar bracing under the carried weight.
Segmental lumbar stabilization against load-induced perturbation.
Prevents contralateral pelvic drop during weight-bearing gait phases.
Controls knee flexion/extension and shock absorption at loading response.
Push-off propulsion at terminal stance under load.
Sustained plantarflexion and postural support during stance.
Assist hip extension and control forward trunk lean.
Supports the loaded shoulder straps and helps stabilize the scapula/shoulder girdle — genuinely relevant for a backpack vs a hand-held carry.
Rotational and lateral trunk control resisting sway with each step.
Co-contracts with TrA and external oblique to stiffen the trunk under load.

cues.text: Tighten both shoulder straps and any waist strap so the load sits high and hugs your spine — a low, loose pack forces you to hunch.
Set a light brace: exhale, gently draw the belly in, and keep ribs stacked over pelvis before you take the first step.
Walk tall with eyes forward and a soft, level chin — resist the urge to fold forward at the waist as you fatigue.
Take smooth, even strides and let each glute drive you forward; keep breathing steadily rather than holding your breath.

regressions.name: Lighter / partial load
regressions.description: Start with a nearly empty pack (a couple of water bottles) and short indoor laps, adding weight only once posture stays tall for the full set.

regressions.name: Stationary marched hold
regressions.description: Wear the loaded pack and march in place or hold a tall standing posture for timed bouts instead of walking, reducing balance and propulsion demand.

progressions.name: Heavier load and longer distance
progressions.description: Progressively add weight and extend walking time or distance (e.g., 10 to 20+ minutes) to build trunk and lower-limb endurance and bone-loading stimulus.

progressions.name: Uneven terrain or incline rucking
progressions.description: Add hills, stairs, or outdoor uneven ground to increase hip-extensor, calf, and stabilizer demand and balance challenge.

dosing: 10–20 minutes of continuous walking, 2–4 sessions per week. Begin with a light load (roughly 5–10% of body weight or whatever keeps posture tall) and progress load, time, or terrain gradually as tolerated. Stop or lighten if posture collapses forward or low-back/shoulder discomfort arises.
unmappedWanted: grip-forearm-flexion (only if pack is hand-held rather than worn)

name: Water Bottle Lateral Raise
description: Standing tall, hold a filled water bottle in each hand at your sides. With a soft elbow, raise both arms out to the sides in the frontal plane until they reach about shoulder height, then lower slowly under control. The water bottles supply the light external load.
rationale: This is a light-load frontal-plane shoulder-abduction exercise that targets the middle deltoid with supraspinatus assistance — the prime movers of raising the arm to the side. Surface-EMG studies of lateral raises and scaption consistently show high middle-deltoid and supraspinatus activation, making the movement a well-established choice for building deltoid and rotator-cuff strength and endurance and for restoring functional overhead/reaching capacity.
