Because water bottles keep the load very light, it is especially suited to early- and mid-stage shoulder rehab, deconditioned or older patients, and anyone building tolerance before progressing to dumbbells. The general evidence for lateral raises as a deltoid strengthener is solid; evidence specific to the improvised water-bottle load is limited and rests mainly on the mechanistic equivalence of light dumbbell loading, so it is graded accordingly. One clinical caution: raising into the painful arc or above shoulder height with the thumbs down can provoke subacromial symptoms, so range and load should be kept comfortable.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover of frontal-plane shoulder abduction from ~15° to 90°.
Initiates abduction (0–15°) and stays active with the deltoid throughout, but is a lesser mover than the middle deltoid, not a co-prime mover. Downgraded from primary to secondary.
Assists abduction, particularly if the raise drifts toward the scapular plane.
Assists abduction in the upper range and controls the lowering (eccentric) phase.
Drives scapular upward rotation to keep the glenoid under the rising humerus.
Contributes to scapular upward rotation as the arm elevates.
Part of the upward-rotation force couple; helps prevent shrugging.
Rotator cuff — helps center the humeral head in the glenoid during elevation.
Rotator cuff — assists humeral-head depression/centering against deltoid shear.

cues.text: Lead with your elbows, keeping a soft (slightly bent) elbow and the bottles just below hand height.
Stop at about shoulder height — no higher — to stay out of any pinching arc.
Keep your shoulders down and neck relaxed; don't shrug the bottles up.
Lower slowly over 2–3 seconds rather than letting the arms drop.

regressions.name: Partial-range raise
regressions.description: Raise only to 45–60° (waist-to-chest height) to reduce load and stay well below any painful arc.

regressions.name: Empty-hand or emptier bottles
regressions.description: Perform with no load or half-filled bottles to groove the pattern and build tolerance before adding water.

progressions.name: Fuller / larger bottles
progressions.description: Increase the water volume or use larger bottles to add resistance as strength improves.

progressions.name: Isometric hold at top
progressions.description: Pause and hold at shoulder height for 3–5 seconds each rep to increase deltoid and cuff time under tension.

dosing: 2–3 sets of 12–15 controlled reps, light load (e.g. 500 mL bottles ≈ 0.5 kg each), 30–60 s rest between sets, 2–3 sessions per week. Keep the range pain-free and stop short of any pinching arc.
emgNotes: Lateral raises produce high middle-deltoid activation (commonly >50–70% MVIC near shoulder height) with substantial supraspinatus involvement; upward-rotator activity (serratus anterior, upper/lower trapezius) rises with elevation angle. Peak deltoid demand occurs around 80–90° of abduction.

name: Prone Press-Up (McKenzie)
description: Lie face-down on the floor (no equipment needed) and press your upper body up with your arms while your hips and low back stay relaxed, letting the lumbar spine extend passively.
rationale: This is the core end-range extension exercise of the McKenzie Method (Mechanical Diagnosis and Therapy) for low back pain. It is indicated for patients with a directional preference for extension — those whose leg or back symptoms centralize (move from the leg/buttock toward the midline of the spine) with repeated lumbar extension, a pattern classically seen with discogenic/derangement-type pain. Randomized and cohort evidence supports classifying LBP by directional preference and matching treatment to it: patients treated with their preferred direction show better pain and function outcomes than mismatched or general exercise. The mechanism proposed is a reduction of flexion-biased mechanical stress and, over repeated end-range loading, symptom centralization, which is itself a favorable prognostic sign. It is a safe, self-administered home tool that lets patients self-manage flare-ups. Evidence is graded moderate: real trial support exists for the MDT/directional-preference approach, but effects are specific to the correctly classified subgroup, and extension is contraindicated where symptoms peripheralize or in extension-sensitive presentations (e.g., spinal stenosis, spondylolisthesis).

muscleRoles.notes: Elbow extension drives the trunk upward; in a true McKenzie press-up the arms, not the spinal extensors, produce the motion. Correct as primary.
Assists the closed-chain press of the trunk over fixed hands. Reasonable synergist.
Assists the pressing action through the shoulder/arm. Reasonable synergist.
Correctly NOT labeled a mover. Should stay relaxed to allow passive end-range lumbar extension; only light postural stabilization. Honest call, kept.
Anterior trunk wall is genuinely stretched as the lumbar spine extends. Valid antagonist lengthening.
Anterolateral trunk wall lengthened during lumbar extension. Valid.
Lengthened over the front/side of the trunk during extension. Valid.

cues.text: Keep your hips, buttocks, and low back completely relaxed and let your arms do all the work — the belly and pelvis stay resting on the floor.
Press up only as far as pain allows, then lower back down; aim to gain a little more range with each repetition as symptoms permit.
Watch where your symptoms go — if pain moves out of the leg and toward the center of your back (centralizes), that is the right direction; stop and reassess if it moves down the leg.
Exhale as you press up to help the low back settle into the sag position.

regressions.name: Prone lying / prone on elbows (prone prop)
regressions.description: Lie face-down flat, or prop on the forearms, and simply rest there to tolerate a gentle sustained extension before attempting full press-ups.

regressions.name: Standing lumbar extension (hands on back)
regressions.description: Stand and lean backward with hands supporting the low back — an easier, weight-bearing way to introduce extension when lying prone is too provocative.

progressions.name: Press-up with belt fixation / self-overpressure
progressions.description: Add a belt or partner overpressure across the pelvis, or exhale and sag further at end range, to achieve greater end-range lumbar extension.

progressions.name: Press-up with hips off-center (side glide correction)
progressions.description: Shift the hips away from the painful side before pressing up to address a lateral shift/asymmetrical derangement.

dosing: Typically 10 repetitions per set, holding the top position 1-2 seconds, repeated roughly every 2-3 hours (about 6-8 sessions per day) during an acute extension-responsive episode; reduce frequency as symptoms centralize and resolve.

name: Hollow Body Hold
description: Lie supine on the floor (or an exercise mat), flatten your low back into the ground, then lift the shoulder blades, arms, and straight legs a few inches and hold that dished, banana-shaped position isometrically.
rationale: The hollow body hold is an anti-extension trunk-stabilization isometric that trains the rectus abdominis, obliques, and transversus abdominis to resist lumbar hyperextension while the hip flexors hold the legs elevated — the exact demand needed to maintain a neutral/braced spine during lifting, carrying, and overhead tasks. Trunk/core stabilization exercise broadly has moderate evidence for reducing pain and disability in chronic non-specific low back pain, and EMG work consistently shows dished, long-lever supine holds produce high abdominal activation. However, evidence specific to the hollow body hold as a named exercise is limited (it comes largely from gymnastics and general strength-and-conditioning practice rather than RCTs), so it is best justified mechanistically: it teaches posterior pelvic tilt control, rib-down bracing, and endurance of the anterior core, which are trainable, transferable qualities. Because the load is scalable simply by changing limb lever length, it is a safe, equipment-free home option for building anterior-core endurance.

muscleRoles.notes: Chief anti-extension trunk flexor holding the dished position; highest isometric demand and the sole true primary.
Assists rib depression and posterior pelvic tilt to flatten the lumbar spine; a supporting contributor, not co-primary with rectus abdominis in this anti-extension hold.
Works with external oblique and TrA to compress and stabilize the trunk.
Provides deep intra-abdominal bracing to maintain the neutral/flexed spine.
Holds the straight legs elevated in isometric hip flexion throughout the hold.
Biarticular assist to hip flexion while keeping the knees extended for the long lever.
Isometrically maintains knee extension so the legs stay straight.
Holds the arms overhead/off the floor in shoulder flexion.
Antagonist held in a lengthened, controlled position as the exercise resists lumbar extension and flattens the lordosis.

cues.text: Press your low back flat into the floor so there is no gap under your spine before you lift anything.
Draw your ribs down toward your pelvis and keep them there — do not let the ribcage flare.
Reach your fingertips and toes long in opposite directions, as if being stretched between two walls.
Lower the arms and legs only as far as you can keep the low back pinned; raise them if the back arches.

regressions.name: Tuck (bent-knee) hollow hold
regressions.description: Keep the knees bent and thighs stacked over the hips to shorten the lever, reducing the anti-extension load while still training the same bracing pattern.

regressions.name: Arms-down / single-limb hollow
regressions.description: Rest the arms alongside the body and extend only one leg at a time, halving the lever load so a beginner can keep the low back flat.

progressions.name: Long-lever low hold
progressions.description: Extend arms fully overhead and lower straight legs closer to the floor, increasing the anti-extension moment while maintaining a flat low back and longer hold times.

progressions.name: Hollow body rock
progressions.description: From the full hollow position, rock the whole rigid body back and forth in a smooth arc, adding a dynamic anti-extension endurance and control challenge.

dosing: Hold 20-30 seconds for 3-4 sets, resting 45-60 seconds between; progress by adding time (up to ~45-60 s) or lengthening the limb levers before adding sets. Perform 3-4 days per week. Stop or regress the moment the low back lifts off the floor.
emgNotes: Dished, long-lever supine holds elicit high rectus abdominis and oblique activation; abdominal EMG rises as the arm/leg levers lengthen and the legs are lowered toward the floor, while erector spinae stays relatively quiet as an isometric anti-extension task.

name: Bodyweight Hip Hinge
description: Standing hip-dominant bend performed with bodyweight only, optionally holding a broomstick vertically against the spine (touching the back of the head, mid-back, and tailbone) as a tactile cue for maintaining a neutral spine while the hips travel backward.
rationale: The hip hinge is the foundational movement pattern for safely picking objects up off the floor, and grooving it is a core goal of home programs for low-back pain and lifting-related injury prevention. Teaching a patient to load through hip flexion/extension while keeping the lumbar spine neutral shifts demand onto the powerful glutes and hamstrings and away from repeated end-range lumbar flexion, reducing shear and compressive strain on the passive spinal tissues. Motor-control and movement-pattern exercise for chronic low-back pain has moderate systematic-review support (comparable in effect to other active exercise), and the bodyweight hinge is the low-risk, no-equipment entry point that must be mastered before loading a Romanian deadlift or kettlebell swing. The broomstick 3-point-contact cue provides real-time proprioceptive feedback shown in motor-learning practice to improve a patient's ability to self-detect and correct lumbar rounding. Evidence for this specific bodyweight drill in isolation is largely mechanistic/expert-based, so it is best framed as a skill-acquisition and pattern-training tool rather than a strength stimulus.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime hip extensor driving the return to standing from the hinged position.
Extend the hip and control the eccentric descent; lengthen under tension as the torso tips forward.
Ischiocondylar (hamstring) portion contributes to hip extension; a distinct muscle not captured by the hamstrings group.
Isometric contraction resists lumbar/thoracic flexion to keep the spine neutral throughout the hinge.
Segmental lumbar stabilization maintaining neutral spine under the forward-tipping moment.
Stabilizes the lumbar spine and pelvis against the forward-bending moment.
Intra-abdominal bracing to protect the spine during the hinge.

cues.text: Push your hips straight back toward the wall behind you and let your chest tip forward like a drawbridge — this is a hinge, not a squat, so the knees only bend slightly.

Hold a broomstick vertically along your spine so it touches the back of your head, your mid-back, and your tailbone; keep all three contact points on the stick the entire time to guarantee a neutral spine.
Keep your weight in your heels and feel a gentle stretch build in the back of your thighs as you lower — stop hinging when you feel that stretch or your spine starts to round.
Stand back up by squeezing your glutes and driving your hips forward to a tall finish; don't lean back or arch your low back at the top.

regressions.name: Hip Hinge to a Chair
regressions.description: Place a chair or box behind you and hinge back until your buttocks lightly tap it, then stand. The target reduces range and reassures the patient about how far to travel, making the pattern easier to feel.

regressions.name: Wall Hip Hinge
regressions.description: Stand a few inches in front of a wall with your back to it and push your hips back to tap the wall, then return. The short lever and clear endpoint groove the backward hip motion with minimal balance and spinal-control demand.

progressions.name: Staggered-Stance (Kickstand) Hinge
progressions.description: Shift most of your weight onto one leg with the other foot lightly staggered behind for balance, and hinge on the loaded leg. This increases unilateral glute and hamstring demand and adds a balance challenge while still bodyweight-only.

progressions.name: Loaded Hip Hinge / Romanian Deadlift
progressions.description: Once the neutral-spine pattern is automatic, hold a dumbbell, kettlebell, or a weighted backpack and perform the same hinge to add a genuine strength stimulus and progress toward the deadlift.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 8-12 controlled reps, 3-4 days/week; focus on quality of the neutral-spine pattern rather than fatigue. A 2-3 second lowering (eccentric) phase reinforces motor control.
emgNotes: Hip-dominant hinging preferentially recruits gluteus maximus and the hamstring group as prime movers while the erector spinae and multifidus act isometrically to hold a neutral spine; deeper hinge angles increase posterior-chain (hamstring/glute) activation relative to a squat pattern.

name: Wall Sit Isometric
description: Stand with your back flat against a wall, then slide down until your hips and knees are bent toward 90 degrees, as if sitting in an invisible chair, and hold the position. The wall is the only equipment needed — it supports the spine and lets you grade depth by how far you slide down. Thighs work statically to keep you from sliding while the trunk stays tall against the wall.
rationale: Two evidence-backed reasons to include this at home. First, cardiovascular: isometric wall squats are the single best-studied isometric protocol for lowering resting blood pressure. A 2023 network meta-analysis of exercise and blood pressure (Edwards et al., British Journal of Sports Medicine) found isometric exercise training produced the largest reductions in systolic and diastolic pressure of any modality, with wall-sit protocols (typically 4 x 2-minute holds, 2-minute rests, 3x/week) among the most effective — making this a practical, equipment-free adjunct for stage-1 hypertension. Second, musculoskeletal: sustained quadriceps isometrics build knee-extensor strength with minimal joint movement and are a well-supported entry point for patellofemoral pain and early knee osteoarthritis, where isometric loading is often better tolerated than dynamic squatting and can produce short-term analgesia. It is a safe, self-dosing home exercise that requires no load and lets the patient control intensity by squat depth and hold time.

muscleRoles.notes: Isometric knee-extensor torque holds the ~90-degree flexed knee against the bodyweight/gravity moment — the defining working group of a wall sit.
Main monoarticular knee extensor; strongly loaded through the static hold.
Largest knee-extension force contributor during the isometric hold.
Bumped from synergist to primary for consistency — the three vasti are co-equal monoarticular knee extensors and all bear the hold; singling VI out as lesser was inconsistent.
Biarticular; with the hip held flexed it works at an active-insufficiency (shortened) disadvantage, so it assists knee extension but is not a lead mover. Synergist is correct.

Downgraded from secondary. The trunk is supported vertically by the wall and the external hip-extension moment is small, so EMG glute demand in a wall sit is low relative to a free squat — it stabilizes the flexed hip/pelvis rather than acting as a secondary mover.
Co-contract to stabilize the flexed knee during the static hold. Stabilizer is appropriate.
Downgraded from synergist. Its hip-extension contribution is minor in this supported, low-hip-torque position; better characterized as a thigh-alignment stabilizer.
Crosses the knee and ankle; provides minor knee/ankle stabilization as the feet press into the floor.
Low demand because the wall supports the trunk, but contributes to maintaining upright spinal posture. Kept as stabilizer.
Provides trunk stiffness so the low back stays in contact with the wall. Stabilizer is fine.

cues.text: Set your feet about shoulder-width apart and one to two foot-lengths out from the wall, so that when you slide down your knees stack over your ankles, not past your toes.
Slide down until your thighs are heading toward parallel with the floor — aim for roughly a 90-degree bend at the hips and knees, but stop higher if that is too intense.
Drive your whole back and low ribs into the wall and press evenly through both heels so the thighs, not the wall, carry the load.
Keep breathing steadily through the hold — do not hold your breath, which spikes blood pressure and defeats the purpose.

regressions.name: Higher wall sit (shallow angle)
regressions.description: Slide down only partway so the knees bend to about 30 to 45 degrees. The shorter lever dramatically reduces quadriceps demand — a good starting point for painful knees or deconditioned patients.

regressions.name: Shorter interval holds
regressions.description: Hold for 10 to 20 seconds and repeat several times with rest between, rather than one long hold, to build tolerance before progressing to 2-minute holds.

progressions.name: Single-leg wall sit
progressions.description: From a two-legged wall sit, extend one leg straight out so the working leg bears the full load isometrically. Substantially increases knee-extensor demand and adds a balance and pelvic-control challenge.

progressions.name: Weighted wall sit
progressions.description: Hold a dumbbell, kettlebell, or heavy household object on the thighs, or place a resistance band around the knees to resist adduction, increasing quadriceps and hip-stabilizer load.

dosing: Blood-pressure protocol: 4 holds of 2 minutes at a moderate intensity (about knee angle giving a hard-but-sustainable effort), with 2 minutes rest between holds, 3 sessions per week for at least 8 to 12 weeks. Strengthening/knee-pain protocol: 3 to 5 holds of 30 to 60 seconds, 1 to 2 minutes rest, most days of the week; progress hold time and depth as tolerated. Stop a hold if knee pain exceeds a mild 3/10 or if you cannot keep breathing normally.
emgNotes: Sustained submaximal quadriceps activation dominates, with vastus medialis and vastus lateralis showing rising EMG amplitude and progressive motor-unit recruitment as the hold continues and fatigue accumulates; deeper knee angles and single-leg variants raise activation. Gluteus maximus and hamstrings show lower-level co-contraction for hip and knee stabilization.

name: Doorframe Calf Stretch
description: Standing facing a doorway, place both hands on the doorframe at chest height and step one leg back. Keep the back knee straight and the heel flat on the floor, then lean your hips toward the doorframe until you feel a stretch through the back of the calf. Bend the back knee slightly to shift the stretch lower toward the Achilles/soleus.
rationale: This stretch targets the gastrocnemius-soleus complex to restore ankle dorsiflexion range of motion, a common limitation that alters gait mechanics and loads the plantar fascia and Achilles tendon. Gastrocnemius-soleus stretching is a first-line, guideline-endorsed component of conservative care for plantar heel pain (plantar fasciitis), where reduced dorsiflexion is a recognized risk factor, and it is also used to address gastrocnemius tightness contributing to Achilles and forefoot overload. Evidence for stretching as a standalone cure is modest, but it reliably improves short-term dorsiflexion ROM and pain when combined with other measures, and the straight-knee versus bent-knee variation lets the patient bias the two-joint gastrocnemius versus the single-joint soleus. It requires only a doorway, making it easy to perform consistently at home.

muscleRoles.notes: Primary two-joint target; stretched maximally with the back knee straight and heel down.
Deeper single-joint plantarflexor; biased when the back knee is slightly bent.
Small two-joint plantarflexor lengthened alongside the gastrocnemius; minor contributor.
Deep posterior compartment placed on stretch as the ankle dorsiflexes; minor.
Lengthened with dorsiflexion, more so with toes kept flat/extended; minor.
Deep plantarflexor lengthened during the dorsiflexion stretch; minor.

Antagonist dorsiflexor; only an active-assist via reciprocal inhibition if the patient consciously dorsiflexes. In a passive lean its contribution is negligible. Kept as synergist (not lengthening, since it shortens).

cues.text: Keep the back heel pressed flat into the floor throughout — letting it lift removes the stretch.
Point the back foot straight ahead so the stretch runs down the midline of the calf, not the inner arch.
Lean from the hips toward the doorframe with a tall trunk rather than rounding the low back.
For the upper calf, keep the back knee locked straight; to shift lower to the Achilles, soften that knee a few degrees while keeping the heel down.

regressions.name: Reduced lean / shorter stance
regressions.description: Take a smaller step back and lean in less, reducing the dorsiflexion angle for those with a tight or painful calf or limited ankle mobility.

regressions.name: Bent-knee (soleus-only) version
regressions.description: Keep the back knee bent for the whole hold. This slackens the gastrocnemius and puts a gentler load through the soleus and Achilles, easing acute symptoms.

progressions.name: Elevated forefoot / heel-drop off a step
progressions.description: Place the ball of the foot on a book or step edge and let the heel sink below level to increase end-range dorsiflexion beyond flat-floor stretch.

progressions.name: Loaded eccentric heel drop
progressions.description: Progress from static stretch to slow, controlled heel-lowering off a step to add loaded lengthening — a bridge toward eccentric or heavy-slow-resistance calf/Achilles loading.

dosing: Hold 30 seconds (build to 45-60 s for tight calves), 3-4 repetitions per leg, once or twice daily. Stretch to a firm but comfortable pull, never sharp pain. Perform both the straight-knee (gastrocnemius) and bent-knee (soleus) variations. A common protocol is 3 x 30 s per side, 2-3 times per day, especially before first steps in the morning for plantar heel pain.
emgNotes: No exercise-specific EMG data cited; muscle roles are assigned from functional anatomy of ankle dorsiflexion placing the posterior compartment plantarflexors on stretch.

name: Countertop-Supported Squat
description: Stand facing a sturdy kitchen countertop with feet shoulder-width apart, resting your hands lightly on the edge. Push your hips back and bend your knees to lower into a squat as deep as is comfortable, using the countertop only for balance and to unload a portion of body weight, then drive through your heels and squeeze your glutes to stand back up.
rationale: A supported squat trains the same sit-to-stand pattern that predicts functional independence: repeated closed-chain knee and hip extension builds lower-limb strength and power, which is strongly associated with reduced fall risk and easier chair/toilet transfers in older and deconditioned adults. The countertop provides an upper-limb "handhold" that lets a patient who cannot yet do a free-standing squat or unsupported sit-to-stand still load the quadriceps and glutes safely, then progressively wean off the arms as strength returns — a graded loading strategy well supported for knee osteoarthritis, post-surgical quad weakness, and general frailty/sarcopenia. Evidence for progressive resistance and sit-to-stand-style training improving lower-limb strength and physical function in older adults is robust; the specific "hands on counter" regression is standard clinical practice rather than a separately trialed protocol, so it is rated moderate.

muscleRoles.notes: Concentric knee extension to rise; eccentric control on descent. Prime mover.
Primary hip extensor driving the stand-up phase.
Component of the quadriceps group; terminal knee extension and patellar tracking. Redundant with quadriceps-group entry.
Component of the quadriceps group; knee extension. Redundant with quadriceps-group entry.
Biarticular quadriceps head; contributes to knee extension. Redundant with quadriceps-group entry.
Assist hip extension and co-contract to stabilize the knee.
Acts as a hip extensor in the deeper squat position.
Controls frontal-plane hip/knee alignment, preventing knee valgus.
Controls the tibia over the fixed foot.
Assists tibial/ankle control in the closed chain.
Maintains a neutral trunk against the forward-lean torque.
Provides intra-abdominal bracing for trunk control.

cues.text: Keep your hands light on the countertop — use it for balance, not to pull yourself up. Let your legs do the work.
Push your hips back as if reaching your seat toward a chair behind you, keeping your chest tall.
Track your knees out over your second and third toes; do not let them cave inward.
Drive down through your heels and squeeze your glutes to stand all the way up.

regressions.name: Higher / partial-range squat
regressions.description: Lower only a few inches (mini-squat) and rely more on the countertop to unload body weight; ideal when strength or knee pain limits depth.

regressions.name: Countertop-assisted sit-to-stand
regressions.description: Place a chair behind you and squat down only to touch the seat, using the counter for support, so the chair caps the depth and provides a safe target.

progressions.name: Fingertip-only / hands-off squat
progressions.description: Progress from full hand support to fingertips, then to hovering hands, and finally a free-standing bodyweight squat as balance and strength improve.

progressions.name: Tempo or single-leg-biased squat
progressions.description: Add a slow 3-4 second eccentric lowering, a pause at the bottom, or shift weight toward one leg to increase load without adding external weight.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 8-12 repetitions, controlled tempo (about 2 seconds down, 2 seconds up), 3-4 days per week. Progress by reducing hand support before adding range, tempo, or reps. Work within a pain-free or mild (≤3/10) range for knee OA or post-surgical cases.

name: Chair Triceps Dip
description: Sit at the edge of a sturdy chair, grip the front edge with hands beside your hips, slide your hips off the seat, then bend and straighten your elbows to lower and press your body up using a chair.
rationale: A closed-chain, bodyweight way to load the triceps and anterior shoulder for the everyday task of pushing the body up off a surface (rising from a chair with armrests, pushing off a bed or the floor). Surface-EMG studies of dip and dip-style pressing consistently rank them among the highest triceps brachii activators, so the exercise reliably trains the elbow extensors and clavicular pectoralis/anterior deltoid that drive functional pressing. Direct randomized evidence for the seated chair variation as a clinical intervention is limited, so the primary justification is mechanistic and functional: it builds upper-limb pressing strength with no equipment, useful for reconditioning after upper-extremity deconditioning and for older adults who rely on arm push-off for transfers. It is contraindicated or should be regressed for anyone with anterior shoulder pain or impingement, since deep bottom positions load the shoulder in extension.
bodyPosition: seated-supported (hands on chair edge, hips off seat, weight through arms)

muscleRoles.notes: Concentric elbow extension to press the body up; eccentrically controls the lowering phase. Correct primary mover for this closed-chain dip.
Assists triceps in elbow extension and stabilizes the elbow joint.
Drives shoulder flexion to raise the torso out of the bottom (shoulder-extended) position. Genuine secondary mover.
Clavicular fibers assist shoulder flexion during the press; sternal fibers also contribute. Reasonable secondary mover.
Maintains scapular depression so the shoulders do not shrug under load. Appropriate stabilizer, not a mover.
Stabilizes the scapula against the thorax throughout the dip. Appropriate stabilizer.

cues.text: Keep your hips close to the chair so your weight stays over your hands, not out in front of you.
Point your elbows straight back as you bend — don't let them flare out to the sides.
Lower only until your upper arms are about parallel to the floor; stop sooner if you feel any pinching in the front of the shoulder.
Keep your shoulders down and away from your ears, and press the floor away through straight arms at the top.

regressions.name: Bent-knee foot placement
regressions.description: Keep knees bent with feet flat and close to the chair so your legs share the load, and use a smaller range of motion.

regressions.name: Bench-supported partial dip
regressions.description: Reduce depth to a few inches or keep hips resting lightly on the seat edge, moving mostly through the arms without full body descent.

progressions.name: Long-lever straight-leg dip
progressions.description: Extend the legs straight out with heels on the floor to shift more bodyweight onto the arms and increase demand.

progressions.name: Feet-elevated dip
progressions.description: Place the heels on a second chair or step so a greater share of bodyweight loads the triceps and shoulders through a fuller range.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 8-12 controlled repetitions, 2-3 times per week, resting 60-90 seconds between sets; use a range of motion that stays pain-free at the front of the shoulder.
emgNotes: Dip-pattern pressing is among the highest triceps brachii activators on surface EMG; anterior deltoid and clavicular pectoralis major contribute during the shoulder-flexion drive, while lower trapezius and serratus anterior act isometrically to keep the scapula depressed and stable.
unmappedWanted: shoulder-flexion (concentric driver at the top of the press was better captured by shoulder-extension for the loaded bottom position)

name: Bird Dog to Elbow-Knee
description: On the floor (a mat or carpet cushions the knees), start in quadruped with hands under shoulders and knees under hips. Reach the opposite arm forward and leg back to torso height, then slowly draw that elbow and knee together beneath your body before re-extending. Requires no equipment beyond your bodyweight and the floor.
rationale: The bird dog is one of McGill's "Big 3" spine-stabilization exercises and is a staple of motor-control programs for non-specific low back pain. Published EMG work shows it produces meaningful multifidus and erector spinae activation while keeping lumbar compressive load relatively low, making it a good choice for training endurance and neuromuscular control of the deep spinal stabilizers without provoking symptoms. Prospective and clinical data support trunk-muscle endurance and lumbar motor control as protective against recurrent low back pain, and bird dog variations feature in guideline-concordant exercise therapy. The "to elbow-knee" dynamic adds an active flexion phase that increases rectus abdominis and oblique demand and trains hip flexor/extensor reciprocation and anti-rotation control through range — a sensible functional progression, though direct trials on this specific variant are sparse, so its added benefit over the static hold rests on mechanistic reasoning rather than dedicated evidence.
equipment: exercise mat or padded floor (optional, for knee comfort)

muscleRoles.notes: Target muscle: segmental lumbar stabilization resisting rotation/holding neutral spine through the extend phase. Works largely isometrically, but is the intended training focus, so primary is defensible for this stabilization drill.
Holds trunk extension and eccentrically controls the transition into/out of the flexion tuck. Primary trained extensor for this variant.
Prime mover for hip extension of the reaching leg (low bodyweight load, but the driving mover).
Prime mover of the active trunk flexion that brings elbow toward knee in the tuck — the feature that distinguishes this from the static bird dog.
Assists trunk flexion and provides anti-rotation/anti-pelvic-drop control.
Contributes to trunk flexion and anti-rotation bracing.
Abdominal bracing / intra-abdominal pressure. Functionally a deep stabilizer; secondary is acceptable.
Primary hip flexor of the tuck; low external load keeps demand modest, so secondary is reasonable.
Assists hip flexion during the elbow-to-knee tuck.
Shoulder flexion to reach the arm forward and hold it in the extended position.
Assist gluteus maximus in hip extension of the reaching leg.
Resists lateral pelvic tilt / lateral flexion, keeps pelvis square.
Stabilizes the pelvis on the stance side against drop and rotation.
Scapular stabilization of the reaching arm.
Closed-chain scapulothoracic stabilization on the weight-bearing arm.

cues.text: Set up with hands directly under your shoulders and knees under your hips; brace your abdominals and find a neutral spine as if balancing a glass of water on your low back.
Reach the opposite arm forward and leg back until both are level with your torso — no higher — without letting your low back arch or your hips rotate open.
Slowly draw that elbow and knee together underneath your body, rounding the spine gently and exhaling as they meet.
Re-extend smoothly to the level reach; move at a controlled pace and keep the pelvis square the whole time rather than dumping into your low back.

regressions.name: Bird Dog Hold (no tuck)
regressions.description: Extend the opposite arm and leg to torso height and hold a stable neutral spine for 5-10 seconds, omitting the elbow-knee crunch. Builds the stabilization base before adding dynamic movement.

regressions.name: Single-limb reach
regressions.description: Extend only one leg (or only one arm) at a time while the other three points stay on the floor, then add the elbow-knee tuck. The wider base lowers the balance and anti-rotation demand.

progressions.name: End-range pauses
progressions.description: Add a 2-3 second hold at both full extension and full elbow-to-knee contact, increasing time under tension and control at each end of the range.

progressions.name: Loaded or unstable variation
progressions.description: Add a light ankle/wrist weight, or place a folded towel or foam pad under the down knee and hand to raise the stability and anti-rotation challenge.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 8-12 controlled reps per side, alternating or completing one side before switching. Move slowly (about 2-3 s per phase), pause briefly at the level reach, and stop a set if you can no longer keep the pelvis square and spine neutral. Perform most days as part of a core motor-control routine.
emgNotes: The static bird dog shows moderate multifidus and erector spinae activation (commonly ~20-40% MVIC in EMG studies) at relatively low lumbar compressive load; the elbow-knee flexion phase adds rectus abdominis and oblique activation not seen in the static hold. Values for this specific dynamic variant are not well characterized in the literature.

name: Prone Y-T-W Raise
description: Lie face down on the floor (a carpet or exercise mat for comfort), arms hanging off to the sides. With thumbs pointing up, lift the arms just off the ground to trace three shapes in sequence — a Y (arms overhead in a wide V), a T (arms straight out to the sides), and a W (elbows bent, drawn down toward the ribs) — leading each lift with the shoulder blades rather than the hands. No equipment beyond the floor is needed.
rationale: This is a foundational home exercise for people with scapular dyskinesis, rounded-shoulder posture, or subacromial/rotator-cuff-related shoulder pain, where the goal is to strengthen the scapular upward rotators and posterior cuff while down-training an overactive upper trapezius. Surface-EMG research on prone scapular exercises (e.g., the body of work by Ekstrom and colleagues in JOSPT and by Cools and colleagues on scapular rehabilitation) consistently shows that prone arm raises at these arm angles elicit high lower- and middle-trapezius and rotator-cuff activation with a favorable lower-trap-to-upper-trap ratio — the pattern clinicians target to restore balanced scapular control. The evidence is strongest at the level of muscle-activation (EMG) and expert consensus; direct randomized outcome trials for this specific three-position drill are more limited, so it is best framed as a well-supported activation and motor-control exercise rather than a proven stand-alone cure. Because it needs no equipment and is easy to self-dose, it is a practical daily posture/shoulder-health drill for a home program.

muscleRoles.notes: Scapular upward rotation/depression; key target especially in the Y. High EMG in prone raises.
Scapular retraction, dominant in the T position.
Shoulder horizontal abduction/extension driving the arm lift in T and W.
Assists scapular retraction and downward rotation in the T and W.
External rotation, emphasized in the W and stabilizing the humeral head.
External-rotation partner to infraspinatus during the W.
Contributes to humeral elevation/abduction in the Y and T.
Couples with lower trapezius for scapular upward rotation in the overhead Y; note EMG activation in prone is typically modest, so synergist (not higher) is appropriate.
Holds a neutral, gently extended prone trunk so lift comes from the shoulder girdle, not lumbar hyperextension.
Intentionally minimized ('no shrug') to bias lower trapezius; retained as stabilizer since no antagonist/down-trained role label is available.
Stretched antagonist as the arms move into horizontal abduction and external rotation.

cues.text: Before you lift, gently glide your shoulder blades down and back toward your opposite hip pockets — start the motion from the blades, not the hands.
Keep thumbs pointing to the ceiling so the shoulders stay externally rotated, and lift the arms only a few inches — height comes from the scapula, not from swinging the arm.
Keep your neck long with your forehead down and your ribs relaxed onto the floor; do not shrug toward your ears or arch your low back to cheat the lift.
Move slowly and pause about 2 seconds at the top of each letter — Y, then T, then W — lowering with control between each.

regressions.name: One letter at a time, no hold
regressions.description: Perform only the T (or whichever position is easiest and pain-free) for a set, lifting just an inch and lowering immediately without the isometric hold, until endurance and control improve.

regressions.name: Standing wall version
regressions.description: Stand facing a wall and slide the arms into the Y, T, and W shapes against the wall (like a wall slide). Reducing the effect of gravity lowers the load while grooving the same scapular pattern.

progressions.name: Longer isometric holds
progressions.description: Increase the hold at the top of each letter to 5-10 seconds, building lower-trapezius endurance without adding load.

progressions.name: Add light household weights
progressions.description: Hold a small filled water bottle or light can in each hand, or perform the raises chest-supported over the edge of a bed or bench for greater range, increasing resistance as control is maintained.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 8-12 slow repetitions of the full Y-T-W sequence (or per letter), with a 2-second hold at the top of each position, performed 3-4 days per week. Progress by lengthening the holds before adding light external load.
emgNotes: Prone arm raises at these angles are used specifically to bias the lower and middle trapezius and posterior rotator cuff while keeping upper-trapezius activity low (favorable lower-trap/upper-trap ratio). The 'thumbs up, no shrug, lead with the shoulder blade' cueing is what shifts activation toward the intended stabilizers; letting the shoulders shrug recruits the upper trapezius and defeats the purpose.

name: Standing Hip Abduction (Counter Support)
description: Stand tall beside a kitchen countertop, resting one hand on it for balance. Keeping the moving leg straight and the toes pointing forward, lift that leg directly out to the side about 30 to 45 degrees without leaning your trunk, then lower under control. The countertop supplies just enough support to isolate the hip rather than the arm.
rationale: Targets the hip abductors — chiefly gluteus medius — which are the key frontal-plane pelvic stabilizers during single-limb stance and gait. Hip abductor weakness is consistently associated with patellofemoral pain, gluteal tendinopathy, hip osteoarthritis, chronic ankle instability, and Trendelenburg gait, and abductor-strengthening programs have moderate evidence for reducing pain and improving function in patellofemoral pain and hip OA. It is also relevant to fall prevention in older adults, since lateral stability and hip abductor strength predict balance and lateral step recovery. Standing weight-bearing abduction is more functionally specific than side-lying work because the stance limb must simultaneously stabilize the pelvis, though EMG studies show side-lying and banded variants generate higher gluteus medius activation, so this counter-supported version is best framed as an accessible entry-level and balance-friendly option that can be progressed. Evidence is set to moderate for abductor strengthening improving the associated conditions, while the specific superiority of this exact standing variant is more limited.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover of the lifting leg's abduction; also works isometrically on the stance leg to keep the pelvis level.
Assists abduction and anterior pelvic/femoral stabilization.
Contributes to abduction, especially with slight hip flexion; keep toes forward to bias glute med over TFL.
Controls trunk/pelvis so motion stays at the hip rather than hiking the pelvis; not a prime mover, so stabilizer is correct.
Upper fibers assist abduction and hold the hip in neutral rotation/extension; secondary to glute med, so stabilizer is appropriate.
Antagonist of the moving leg; lengthens as the leg abducts and provides eccentric control on the return.

cues.text: Stand tall and lift the leg straight out to the side, stopping around 30 to 45 degrees before the pelvis starts to hike.
Keep the kneecap and toes pointing forward the whole time — do not let the leg drift into flexion or the foot turn out.
Rest the hand on the counter for balance only; drive the motion from the side of your hip, not by pushing on the counter.
Keep the trunk upright and level — imagine your shoulders and hips staying square rather than leaning away from the moving leg.

regressions.name: Reduced range of motion
regressions.description: Lift the leg only 10 to 20 degrees, moving within a comfortable, controlled range while building strength and confidence.

regressions.name: Two-hand counter support
regressions.description: Face the counter and hold with both hands for greater stability if single-hand support feels unsteady.

progressions.name: Fingertip or no support
progressions.description: Progress to light fingertip contact, then hands-free, adding a balance challenge on the stance leg.

progressions.name: Resistance band or ankle weight
progressions.description: Loop a band around the ankles or add a light ankle cuff weight to increase gluteus medius demand toward strengthening loads.

dosing: 2 to 3 sets of 10 to 15 repetitions per leg, performed slowly (about 2 seconds up, 2 seconds down), 3 to 4 days per week. Progress by adding a band or ankle weight once 15 controlled reps are easy with a level pelvis.
emgNotes: Standing/weight-bearing hip abduction produces moderate gluteus medius activation but generally lower than side-lying abduction, side-plank, and banded lateral-walk variants in comparative EMG studies; activation increases with added band or ankle-weight resistance and when the toes are kept forward to reduce TFL substitution.

name: Calf Raise off Stair Edge
description: Standing on a stair with the balls of both feet on the step edge and heels hanging off, rise onto the toes then lower the heels below the edge through full range. Hold the handrail lightly for balance only.
rationale: This trains the plantarflexors (gastrocnemius/soleus) and loads the Achilles tendon through a large range, including the lengthened position as the heel drops below the step edge — something a flat-floor heel raise cannot achieve. Plantarflexor strength and calf-muscle capacity are prime movers of gait push-off, running, jumping, and stair negotiation, and they decline markedly with age; low plantarflexor strength is associated with slower gait speed and reduced balance/fall resilience in older adults. Progressive heel-raise programs reliably increase plantarflexor strength and calf muscle volume, and full-range/eccentric heel-drop loading off a step is a cornerstone of rehabilitation for Achilles (mid-portion) tendinopathy, where the Alfredson eccentric heel-drop and heavy-slow-resistance calf protocols have solid trial support for reducing pain and improving function. Evidence level set to moderate: strong support exists for calf loading in Achilles tendinopathy and for plantarflexor strengthening generally, though the specific stair-edge dose varies across studies.

muscleRoles.notes: Chief plantarflexor with the knee kept straight; drives the rise onto the toes.
Deep plantarflexor; contributes throughout and dominates when the knee is allowed to bend.
Assists plantarflexion and controls midfoot/arch as the heel drops and lifts.
Assists plantarflexion and stabilizes the first ray/lateral ankle.
Assists plantarflexion and guards against inversion at the extended edge position.
Assists plantarflexion and grips through the great toe at the top of the raise.
Assists plantarflexion and toe flexion for balance on the edge.
Minor plantarflexor accompanying the gastrocnemius.
Holds the knee in extension so load stays on the calf rather than the knee.
Antagonist dorsiflexor; stretched at the top of the raise as the ankle plantarflexes.

cues.text: Place the balls of both feet on the stair edge with heels hanging free; rest a hand on the handrail for balance, not to pull yourself up.
Rise slowly and as high as you can onto the toes, pausing briefly at the top.
Lower under control until the heels sink below the step edge and you feel a gentle stretch through the calves and Achilles.
Keep the knees straight to bias the gastrocnemius, and move smoothly — no bouncing at the bottom.

regressions.name: Flat-floor double-leg heel raise
regressions.description: Stand on level ground on both feet and raise up onto the toes without the heel drop, removing the stretch and balance demand of the edge.

regressions.name: Supported partial-range raise
regressions.description: Hold the handrail more firmly and work only the top half of the range (no heel drop below the edge) until strength and confidence improve.

progressions.name: Single-leg stair-edge calf raise
progressions.description: Perform on one leg at a time to roughly double the load through each plantarflexor complex.

progressions.name: Loaded slow eccentric heel drop
progressions.description: Add a backpack or hold a dumbbell and lower over a slow 3-4 second count into the stretch (Alfredson-style eccentric loading) for tendon capacity.

dosing: 3 sets of 12-15 reps, 3-4x/week, with a controlled ~2-3 s lower and a brief pause in the stretched bottom position; progress to single-leg or added load as tolerated. For Achilles tendinopathy, a higher-volume eccentric heel-drop scheme (e.g., 3x15 twice daily) may be used under guidance.

name: Towel Hamstring Curl
description: Lie face-up with knees bent and heels resting on a folded towel on a smooth floor. Lift into a bridge, then slowly slide the heels away from you until the legs are nearly straight (letting the hamstrings lengthen under load), and drag the towel back in by curling the knees to return. The towel on a slick surface creates the low-friction glide that turns a floor bridge into a sliding knee-flexor curl.
rationale: This is a home-friendly way to load the hamstrings eccentrically — the same training stimulus behind eccentric knee-flexor programs. Eccentric hamstring strengthening (best studied as the Nordic hamstring exercise) has strong RCT and meta-analytic support for reducing hamstring strain injury incidence in athletes and for building fascicle length and eccentric strength, both linked to injury risk. The towel/slider curl is a lower-load, easily scalable regression of that principle: it lets a patient train the hip-hinge-into-knee-flexion eccentric pattern early in rehab or before they can tolerate a full Nordic, using only a towel and floor. It is a good indication for hamstring strain rehab, posterior-chain endurance, and as a bridge toward Nordic curls. Honest caveat: the specific towel/slider variant has limited direct trial evidence — its support is extrapolated from the well-studied eccentric hamstring literature and from EMG showing high hamstring activation in slider/bridge curls — so I have set the evidence level to moderate rather than strong.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover; eccentrically controls the slide-out (knee extension) and concentrically curls the heels back in (knee flexion) while helping hold hip extension.
Lateral hamstring head; principal eccentric contributor and the head most often strained.
Medial hamstring head contributing to knee flexion and hip extension.
Medial hamstring head contributing to knee flexion and hip extension.
Isometrically extends the hip to keep the bridge elevated as the heels slide out.
Two-joint knee flexor; assists dragging the heels back in during the concentric phase.
Maintains a level bridge and resists hip drop during the slide.
Provides trunk/pelvic stability so load stays on the hamstrings rather than the low back.

Antagonist to the hamstring curl; stretched during the concentric curl-in (knee flexion) phase, and held at length at the hip by the extended-hip bridge position. Corrected: it does NOT lengthen during the slide-out — knee extension shortens the quadriceps.

cues.text: Keep your hips lifted the whole time — the tabletop line from knees to shoulders should not sag as your feet slide away.
Slide out slowly, taking 3 to 5 seconds; the effort is in resisting the stretch, not in flinging the heels out.
Drive your heels down into the towel to drag it back in, feeling the back of your thighs do the pulling.
Stop the slide-out at the point where your hips start to drop or you feel it in your low back, and build range from there.

regressions.name: Partial-range double-leg curl
regressions.description: Slide the heels out only part way and stay in a range where the hips stay fully lifted, gradually extending the distance as strength improves.

regressions.name: Bridge with assisted return
regressions.description: Use both legs and lightly push through the arms/heels to help drag the towel back in, reducing the concentric demand on the hamstrings.

progressions.name: Single-leg towel curl
progressions.description: Perform the slide-out and curl-in on one leg with the other foot lifted, roughly doubling the eccentric load on the working hamstring.

progressions.name: Tempo eccentric emphasis / Nordic progression
progressions.description: Slow the slide-out to 5+ seconds for maximal eccentric loading, then progress toward the Nordic hamstring curl for full high-load eccentric training.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 6-10 controlled reps, emphasizing a slow 3-5 second eccentric slide-out, 2-3 times per week on non-consecutive days. Progress by adding range, reps, or moving to single-leg once form and hip height are maintained without low-back strain.

name: Wall Ball Squat
description: Standing with an exercise ball pinned between the low back and a wall (a plain wall works too), slide down until the knees bend toward roughly 90 degrees, then press back up or hold. The ball rolls with the spine to guide a smooth, vertical descent and give proprioceptive feedback; the wall unloads balance so the quads can work in a controlled closed chain.
rationale: This is primarily a closed-chain quadriceps builder that patients can do safely at home with almost no equipment. Quadriceps strengthening is one of the best-supported conservative interventions for knee osteoarthritis and patellofemoral pain, and the wall squat keeps the trunk supported so load can be dosed by squat depth and hold time — useful when balance, pain, or deconditioning limit a free-standing squat. The mid-range wall position also keeps patellofemoral joint stress moderate, which is why it is a common early-stage knee rehab choice. Separately, the isometric wall sit/wall squat has emerging support for lowering resting blood pressure: a 2023 systematic review and meta-analysis in a sports-medicine journal found isometric exercise training — with wall squats among the most effective modes — produced meaningful reductions in resting systolic and diastolic pressure. Evidence is set to moderate: quad strengthening for knee pain is strongly supported, and the blood-pressure benefit is promising but from a smaller, still-maturing body of trials.

muscleRoles.notes: Main mover; eccentric on descent, concentric/isometric to control knee extension in this closed-chain, back-supported squat.
Knee extensor; emphasized near terminal extension and relevant to patellofemoral tracking.
Mono-articular knee extensor, primary contributor.
Corrected from secondary: same mono-articular knee-extension function as the other vasti, works equally in the squat.
Biarticular; contributes at the knee but at a length disadvantage due to concurrent hip flexion.
Assists hip extension on the ascent from the bottom position.
Co-contract to stabilize the knee; hip-extension contribution limited by the supported trunk.
Helps keep knees tracking over toes; frontal-plane demand is modest in this bilateral, wall-supported position.
Stabilizes ankle/knee against forward shin translation.
Maintains trunk position; demand reduced because the back is supported by the ball/wall.
Provides trunk bracing during the hold.

cues.text: Place the ball at your low back against the wall and step your feet out about a foot ahead of your hips, shoulder-width apart.
Slide straight down until your knees reach about 90 degrees — keep shins vertical and knees stacked over your mid-foot, not caving inward.
Drive evenly through both heels and mid-foot to press back up; avoid pushing onto your toes.
Only go as deep as stays pain-free; if the front of the knee complains, come up higher and shorten the hold.

regressions.name: High wall sit
regressions.description: Slide down only partway (knees ~120-140 degrees) and hold briefly, reducing quadriceps and patellofemoral load.

regressions.name: Supported dynamic wall squat
regressions.description: Perform slow up-and-down reps in a shallow range instead of a sustained hold, resting between reps to manage fatigue and pain.

progressions.name: Deeper / longer isometric hold
progressions.description: Progress toward a 90-degree knee angle and build hold time (e.g., 4 x 2-minute holds), the dosing used in isometric blood-pressure protocols.

progressions.name: Single-leg wall sit
progressions.description: Hold the wall-sit position on one leg (other foot lightly off the floor) to markedly increase quadriceps demand and challenge frontal-plane control.

dosing: Strengthening: 3-5 sets of 30-45 second holds at a tolerable knee angle, or 3 sets of 10-15 slow reps, most days of the week. Blood-pressure protocol: 4 x 2-minute holds near 90-135 degrees of knee flexion with 1-2 minutes rest, ~3 sessions per week. Progress by increasing depth, hold time, or advancing to single-leg. Stay within pain-free range.

name: Step-Up on Stairs
description: Standing at the bottom of a staircase, place one whole foot on the first step, then drive through that leg to lift your body up until you are standing on the step; step back down under control and repeat, alternating or completing all reps on one leg. Uses a single household stair (or the bottom step) as the platform.
rationale: Step-ups are a closed-chain, weight-bearing exercise that trains the exact task most patients need to reclaim: negotiating stairs and rising against gravity on one limb. They strongly load the lead-limb quadriceps and gluteals through a functional range and are a staple in knee osteoarthritis, patellofemoral pain, ACL and total-knee rehabilitation, and older-adult conditioning. Like sit-to-stand, they build lower-limb strength and power, which are well-established correlates of gait speed, stair-climbing ability, and fall risk in older adults; because they are unilateral, they also expose and correct side-to-side strength deficits and frontal-plane hip control (gluteus medius) that contribute to knee valgus. Step height directly grades the demand, making this an easily dosed home substitute for leg-press or squat loading. Evidence for progressive lower-limb strengthening improving function and reducing fall risk is robust, though most stair step-up studies are EMG/biomechanics and program components rather than isolated head-to-head trials, so I set the evidence level at moderate.

muscleRoles.notes: Extends the knee of the lead (stepping) leg to lift the body concentrically and controls descent eccentrically.
Extends the hip of the lead leg to raise the trunk over the step; demand rises with step height.
Contributes terminal knee extension and patellar tracking on the lead limb (sub-component of the quadriceps group).
Major knee-extension force producer on the lead limb (sub-component of the quadriceps group).
Assist hip extension and stabilize the knee during the lift.
Controls pelvic drop and frontal-plane knee alignment during single-leg loading.
Assists gluteus medius in hip abduction/pelvic stabilization on the stance limb.
Posterior head assists hip extension from the flexed starting position.
Plantarflexes to help drive the body upward and stabilizes the knee.
Plantarflexion and ankle stability under load through the step.
Maintains an upright trunk against the forward-flexed step position.
Provides trunk/lumbopelvic stability during the single-leg transition.

cues.text: Place your whole foot flat on the step so your heel is supported, not just the ball of the foot.
Drive up by pushing through the heel of the top foot, keeping most of your weight on the stepping leg rather than pushing off the bottom foot.
Keep your kneecap tracking over your second and third toes; do not let the knee collapse inward.
Lower slowly and quietly, tapping the bottom foot lightly instead of dropping onto it.

regressions.name: Hold the rail
regressions.description: Lightly hold the stair rail or wall with one or both hands to share load and provide balance while you build strength and confidence.

regressions.name: Assisted / partial step-up
regressions.description: Use a lower step or push off the bottom foot to help, reducing how much work the top leg does through range.

progressions.name: Slow eccentric step-down
progressions.description: Emphasize a 3-5 second controlled lowering on the working leg to increase eccentric quadriceps and gluteal demand.

progressions.name: Loaded step-up
progressions.description: Hold a backpack, water jugs, or dumbbells, or step onto a higher stair, to progressively increase resistance.

dosing: 2-3 sets of 8-12 repetitions per leg, 2-3 times per week; rest about 60-90 seconds between sets. Start with the bottom step and light hand support, progressing height and load as control and strength improve.
emgNotes: Lead-limb quadriceps (vasti and rectus femoris) and gluteus maximus show the highest activation during the concentric lift; gluteus medius activity rises during single-leg loading to control pelvic drop. Higher step heights and slower eccentric lowering increase quadriceps and gluteal EMG demand.
unmappedWanted: hip-abduction-stabilization (frontal-plane control by gluteus medius is a stabilizing action, not a primary concentric movement here)

name: Seated Knee Extension (Bodyweight)
description: Sit tall on a sturdy chair with feet flat. Straighten one knee to lift the foot until the leg is nearly parallel to the floor, hold briefly at the top, then lower under control. Bodyweight of the shin/foot provides the only resistance.
rationale: This is an open-kinetic-chain quadriceps strengthening exercise, the most isolated way to load the quadriceps (especially the vastii and VMO) without needing equipment. Quadriceps strength is a robust predictor of physical function and is consistently reduced after knee injury, surgery, immobilization, and with age. Progressive quadriceps strengthening is a core, guideline-supported intervention for knee osteoarthritis, where it reliably reduces pain and improves function (a first-line recommendation in OARSI/ACR guidelines), and OKC knee extension is well established for restoring quadriceps strength and controlling arthrogenic muscle inhibition after ACL reconstruction and other knee injury. Because resistance here is only the weight of the shin and foot, it is a low, controllable load well suited to early rehab and deconditioned older adults, and the terminal-range hold specifically recruits the vastus medialis, which is relevant for patellofemoral tracking. One honest caveat: near-full-extension OKC loading increases patellofemoral joint stress, so patients with anterior knee pain often work through a mid-range (roughly 90 to 45 degrees) arc rather than locking out.

muscleRoles.notes: Prime mover of open-chain knee extension against the weight of the shin and foot.
Active knee extensor across the arc; emphasized in terminal range, though it cannot be selectively isolated from the rest of the quadriceps.
Contributes to knee extension throughout the arc.
Deep monoarticular knee extensor active across the full range.
Biarticular; contributes to knee extension but at a length disadvantage with the hip flexed in sitting.
Borderline: helps hold the flexed thigh seated, but the chair provides most of that support, so demand is low.
Antagonists that lengthen at the knee and eccentrically control the lowering phase.
Stretched across the knee as it straightens.
Stretched across the knee as it straightens.
Stretched across the knee as it straightens.

cues.text: Sit tall against the chair back with your thigh fully supported and hands resting at your sides or on the seat.
Squeeze the thigh and slowly straighten your knee, leading with the top of the foot until the leg is nearly level.
Pause and firmly tighten the muscle just above and inside the kneecap for a 2-second hold at the top.
Lower slowly over 3 to 4 seconds rather than letting the leg drop; keep the movement smooth and controlled.

regressions.name: Short-arc quad (bolster)
regressions.description: Place a rolled towel under the knee and extend only through the final 30 to 40 degrees to full straight, reducing load and patellofemoral stress.

regressions.name: Seated isometric quad set
regressions.description: Keep the foot on the floor and simply tighten the quadriceps to gently press the knee straight, holding without lifting the shin.

progressions.name: Ankle-weight knee extension
progressions.description: Add a light ankle weight (or a loaded bag over the foot) to increase resistance as bodyweight becomes easy.

progressions.name: Eccentric-emphasis / single-leg tempo
progressions.description: Lift with both efforts but lower on one leg, or extend the lowering phase to 5 to 6 seconds to overload the eccentric portion.

dosing: 2 to 3 sets of 10 to 15 repetitions per leg, with a 2-second hold at end range, most days of the week; progress load or reps once the target range is completed without fatigue or knee pain.
emgNotes: Vastus medialis activity increases toward terminal extension, supporting the end-range hold; rectus femoris contribution is relatively reduced in sitting because the flexed hip shortens it (active insufficiency).

name: Standing Calf Stretch on Step
description: Stand on a stair or step with the balls of both feet on the edge and heels hanging off the back. Hold the railing or wall for balance and let your heels sink below the step level under body weight to stretch the calf. Keep the back knee straight to bias the gastrocnemius, then bend it slightly to target the deeper soleus.
rationale: The main indication is restoring ankle dorsiflexion range of motion in patients with tight calves — a common finding in plantar heel pain, Achilles tendinopathy, mid-portion Achilles complaints, and post-immobilization stiffness, and a contributor to compensatory gait and forefoot overload. Static calf stretching has moderate evidence for producing short-term gains in dorsiflexion ROM, and gastrocnemius/plantar-fascia stretching programs have moderate RCT support for reducing pain in plantar fasciitis (e.g., first-step pain). The step position is valuable because it uses body weight to load the stretch through greater dorsiflexion than a floor or wall stretch allows, and it separates gastrocnemius (knee straight, since it crosses the knee) from soleus (knee bent) so the patient can address whichever is limiting. Honest caveat: evidence that calf stretching prevents lower-limb injury is limited, and ROM gains are largely acute/short-term unless the program is sustained — so this is best framed as a mobility and symptom-management tool, not a standalone cure.

muscleRoles.notes: Primary stretch target with the knee straight; bi-articular, so knee extension maximizes elongation as the heel drops into dorsiflexion.
Deeper stretch target emphasized with the knee bent, which slackens the gastrocnemius and shifts tension to the mono-articular soleus.
Small bi-articular posterior-compartment muscle stretched along with the gastrocnemius.
Deep posterior plantarflexor/invertor lengthened as the ankle dorsiflexes.
Deep plantarflexor placed under mild stretch through the dorsiflexed ankle, more so if the big toe rests on the step edge.
Deep posterior plantarflexor lengthened with ankle dorsiflexion.
Anterior-compartment dorsiflexor providing low-level reciprocal/eccentric control of the heel drop; gravity supplies most of the stretch force, so its contribution is minor but valid.
Isometrically maintains knee extension in the knee-straight variation to keep tension on the gastrocnemius.
Helps hold the hip and trunk upright over the stance leg during the hold.

cues.text: Place the balls of both feet on the step edge and let your heels drop slowly below the step until you feel a gentle pull in the calf — not pain.
Keep the stretching knee straight and the heel, hip, and shoulder stacked in a straight line; avoid letting the hip sag forward.
Hold the railing or wall lightly for balance so you can relax the calf and let body weight do the work rather than muscling into it.
After the straight-knee hold, softly bend the knee to move the stretch lower into the deep calf (soleus) near the heel cord.

regressions.name: Floor / wall calf stretch (heel down)
regressions.description: Perform a standard standing calf stretch with the heel flat on the floor and hands on a wall, staying off the step edge. Removes the balance and drop-below-neutral demand while still lengthening the calf.

regressions.name: Reduced-depth step stretch
regressions.description: Lower the heels only partway (to level with the step) instead of a full drop, and use two hands on the rail. Cuts the dorsiflexion load for sensitive ankles or early-stage Achilles pain.

progressions.name: Single-leg step calf stretch
progressions.description: Perform the drop on one foot at a time, letting more body weight load a single calf for a deeper stretch and added balance challenge.

progressions.name: Eccentric heel-drop transition
progressions.description: Progress the same step position into slow eccentric heel drops (Alfredson-style) for Achilles tendon loading once mobility and tolerance allow, moving from passive stretch to active strengthening.

dosing: Hold each stretch 30 seconds, 3-4 repetitions per leg, doing both a knee-straight (gastrocnemius) and a slightly knee-bent (soleus) hold. Perform once or twice daily; accumulating roughly 2-3 minutes per calf per session is a reasonable target. Stretch to gentle tension, never sharp pain, and ease off if there is nerve tingling or numbness in the foot.
emgNotes: This is a passive/gravity-driven static stretch, so target muscle EMG activity should stay low; the posterior calf (gastrocnemius, soleus) is under passive tension rather than active contraction. Excessive gripping or high plantarflexor EMG during the hold indicates the patient is guarding and should be cued to relax and let body weight lower the heel.
